title: "Validity is a Theoretical Problem - A Computational Psychometrics Perspective on Cognitive Task Validity"

## Introduction

Why Validity Remains a Foundational Problem Across Cognitive Psychology

Three parallel measurement puzzles confront contemporary cognitive psychology, each revealing the same fundamental challenge: sophisticated psychometric methods cannot resolve what our measures actually measure.

The attention control puzzle. Recent work exemplifies state-of-the-art measurement practice in attention research. @DraheimEtAl2021 systematically optimized reliability through careful scoring methods, employed "toolbox" sampling across diverse conflict task variants (Flanker, Simon, Stroop families), and demonstrated coherent factor structures through confirmatory factor analysis. By conventional psychometric standards, this represents exemplary measurement: high reliability, broad task sampling, convergent validity through factor coherence. Yet fundamental questions persist [@DraheimEtAl2023]. Why do different attention control tasks often fail to correlate substantially [@HedgeEtAl2018; @ReyMermetEtAl2018; @EnkaviEtAl2019]? Why does the same individual sometimes show strong control effects on one task but not another [@ReyMermetEtAl2019]? Why do latent factors extracted from attention tasks show stronger relationships with processing speed than with working memory capacity [@OberauerEtAl2024]?

The working memory capacity puzzle. @WilhelmEtAl2013 titled their comprehensive review "What is working memory capacity, and how can we measure it?" A decade later, the question remains unresolved. @BurgoyneEtAl2024 documented that complex span tasks and N-back tasks—both widely labeled "working memory capacity" measures—correlate weakly despite high within-task reliability. @WilhelmEtAl2025 ask whether working memory capacity can be fractionated into dissociable components or represents an integrated system, noting that measurement approaches cannot resolve this theoretical question. Sophisticated psychometric analyses reveal multiple factors within and across working memory task batteries, but factor structures alone cannot establish what cognitive processes these factors represent. Does a "working memory capacity" factor reflect maintenance capacity, retrieval efficiency, attentional control, resistance to interference, or some combination? The correlation patterns cannot answer.

The processing speed puzzle. Since @Donders1868 introduced the subtraction method to decompose reaction times into component processes, researchers have sought to isolate processing speed components. Modern work continues this effort with sophisticated tools. @SchmitzWilhelm2016 showed that different distributional parameters of reaction time (mean, variance, skewness) show different relationships to intelligence and different developmental trajectories, suggesting they reflect distinct processes. Yet the fundamental problem persists: behavioral RT measures inevitably conflate multiple processes—perceptual encoding, decision processes, response selection, motor execution. No amount of careful psychometric work can decompose these contributions without explicit theory about how they combine to generate observable RTs.

The common pattern. These are not isolated methodological challenges—they reveal a structural problem. Across cognitive domains, sophisticated psychometric practices fail to resolve what tasks actually measure. High reliability does not guarantee valid measurement of intended constructs. Factor coherence demonstrates shared variance but not what psychological mechanisms create it. Weak convergent validity might reflect measurement inadequacy or genuine construct distinctiveness, and correlation patterns cannot adjudicate. Cross-domain construct claims (e.g., "working memory capacity *is* attention control"; @KaneEngle2003; @KaneEtAl2001) rest on moderate behavioral correlations whose interpretation remains theoretically underdetermined.

These persistent difficulties are often interpreted as methodological challenges requiring better psychometric techniques, larger samples, or more careful task selection. However, we argue that the core problem is theoretical rather than methodological. Even when tasks demonstrate excellent psychometric properties, we cannot determine what cognitive processes they measure without explicit theoretical commitments about how those processes generate observable behavior. Factor coherence shows that tasks share variance, but not what psychological mechanisms create that shared variance. High reliability demonstrates measurement consistency, but not construct validity. In the absence of explicit process theory, validation becomes circular: tasks are validated by their correlations with other tasks assumed to measure the same construct.

Operationalizing Borsboom's validity framework. This paper develops the argument that validity in cognitive psychology is fundamentally a theoretical problem that requires explicit models of how cognitive processes generate behavioral data. Building on @Borsboom2004's causal conception of validity—that valid measurement requires attributes to *causally produce* measurement outcomes—we show how computational cognitive models provide practical implementation of this framework. Formal models specify what cognitive processes exist (as model parameters) and how they causally generate observations (through explicit generative mechanisms). Parameter recovery analysis then tests the causal link: whether individual differences in behavioral indicators actually reflect individual differences in the cognitive processes that generated them.

We demonstrate this framework through systematic simulations using conflict tasks as a detailed case study, revealing how validity claims depend on explicit model assumptions. Our simulations show systematic patterns—parameter confounding across indicators, reliability-validity dissociations, and correlation transfer failures from parameters to behavioral measures—that clarify when and why behavioral indicators fail to validly measure cognitive processes. These patterns explain the persistent measurement puzzles across attention, working memory, and processing speed research.

Scope and structure. Our argument proceeds in three parts. First, we diagnose why traditional approaches to validity assessment—particularly those based on cross-task correlations and factor structures—cannot resolve fundamental questions about what tasks measure (Sections 2-3). Second, we develop a framework for model-based validity analysis, clarifying the necessary role of process theory and the advantages of generative cognitive models for validity assessment (Sections 4-9). Third, we demonstrate this framework through systematic simulations showing how validity claims depend on explicit model assumptions (Sections 10-13). We conclude by articulating implications for measurement practice across cognitive domains and addressing concerns about model dependence.

Our goal is not to dismiss psychometric refinement or correlational evidence, nor to adjudicate between specific cognitive theories. Rather, we aim to show that validity claims—statements about what tasks and indicators actually measure—require explicit theoretical commitments that go beyond correlational patterns. The computational psychometrics framework provides tools for making those commitments transparent and testable.

Generalizability. While our empirical demonstrations focus on conflict tasks, the validity principles generalize across cognitive measurement domains. Process impurity (indicators conflating multiple mechanisms), differential validity (different indicators measuring different processes), and correlation transfer failure (parameter-level coherence not guaranteeing indicator-level convergence) are structural features that arise whenever multiple cognitive processes jointly determine behavioral outcomes. We illustrate cross-domain applications throughout, showing how model-based validity analysis addresses measurement puzzles in working memory capacity, processing speed, intelligence research, and cross-domain construct claims. The principles apply broadly to any domain where cognitive processes must be inferred from behavioral observations.

This paper focuses specifically on measurement challenges in process-oriented cognitive psychology—contexts where researchers aim to measure specific cognitive mechanisms (e.g., conflict resolution, maintenance capacity, evidence accumulation rates) rather than purely descriptive individual differences. We do not aim to resolve longstanding debates about the structure of executive functions or the nature of general intelligence. Instead, we use these domains to illuminate a general theoretical problem and demonstrate a principled solution: without explicit models of how constructs generate data, we cannot determine what our measures actually measure. Computational models, by operationalizing @Borsboom2004's causal framework, provide the theoretical foundation for valid cognitive measurement.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Classical Validity Theory

Theory Was Always Central

The role of theory in validity assessment is not a new idea—it has been central to validity frameworks since their inception. However, the specific theoretical requirements for cognitive task validation have often remained implicit or underspecified in practice. Understanding what classical frameworks demanded, and where those demands went unmet, clarifies both the persistent validity challenges in cognitive psychology and the need for more explicit theoretical frameworks. This manuscript provides a practical implementation of @Borsboom2004's causal-theoretical conception of validity through computational cognitive models.

Validity as theory testing. @CronbachMeehl1955 introduced construct validity to address situations where "the attribute or quality measured by a test" is not directly observable and cannot be defined operationally. They argued that construct validation is fundamentally a process of theory testing: the construct of interest is embedded in a "nomological network" of theoretical relationships that generate testable predictions about patterns among measures, experimental manipulations, group differences, and developmental trends. Validation therefore requires integrating multiple lines of evidence under explicit theoretical guidance. Critically, Cronbach and Meehl recognized the risk of circularity: "Unless the network makes contact with observations independent of the test, the investigation is boot-strapping and is not construct validation" (p. 291). The theoretical network must go beyond correlations among measures to specify how the construct relates to other constructs and observable outcomes.

This framework had broad impact across psychological measurement domains. Intelligence research [@Cattell1943; @Cattell1945] used nomological networks to validate ability constructs, working memory research [@EngleEtAl1992; @EngleEtAl1999] grounded capacity measures in theoretical frameworks, and modern executive function research applies construct validation logic to attention control and cognitive flexibility. However, across these domains, the theory-observation link often remained underspecified: what exactly are the cognitive processes, and how do they causally generate the observed behavioral patterns?

Convergent and discriminant validity. @CampbellFiske1959 operationalized key aspects of construct validation through the multitrait-multimethod (MTMM) matrix. Their framework emphasized two requirements: convergent validity (different methods measuring the same construct should correlate highly) and discriminant validity (measures of different constructs should correlate more weakly, even when using the same method). This approach helpfully separated trait variance from method variance and established convergent validity as a key empirical criterion. However, it also embedded a critical assumption: that correlation magnitude can adjudicate whether different measures target the same underlying construct. This assumption becomes problematic when the processes generating observed correlations are complex or incompletely understood—a point to which we return in Section 3.

This logic persists across cognitive domains. Working memory capacity-attention control correlations [@KaneEngle2003; @UnsworthEtAl2004] are interpreted as evidence for shared constructs, but the moderate correlation magnitude (*r* ≈ .3-.5) leaves ambiguous what processes are actually shared. Processing speed-ability correlations [@NeubauserBucik1996] show broad relationships, but which speed processes explain individual differences in which abilities remains theoretically underdetermined. The MTMM logic provides a validation framework, but its implementation requires theoretical specification of what processes different measures engage—a requirement often left implicit.

Unified frameworks and interpretive arguments. Modern validity theorists have refined these ideas while maintaining theory's central role. @Messick1995 argued for a unified conception of validity centered on "score meaning"—what inferences are justified from test performance. Rather than categorizing validity evidence into separate types (content, criterion, construct), Messick emphasized that all evidence contributes to evaluating the quality of inferences and their consequences. @Kane2013 developed this into an argument-based framework: validation requires articulating an explicit interpretation/use argument (IUA) and systematically evaluating the chain of inferences from performance to construct claims. These frameworks emphasize that validity is about the quality of interpretive arguments, but they leave open what constitutes adequate theoretical specification of "score meaning."

Contemporary debates across cognitive domains illustrate this challenge. What is working memory capacity? [@WilhelmEtAl2013; @WilhelmEtAl2025; @BurgoyneEtAl2024]. What is processing speed? [@SchmitzWilhelm2016]. What is attention control? [@OberauerEtAl2024]. These questions about "score meaning" cannot be resolved through additional correlational evidence alone—they require explicit specification of what cognitive processes these constructs represent and how those processes generate observable task performance.

Construct representation versus nomothetic span. @Whitely1983 [subsequently Embretson] made a distinction that proves particularly relevant for cognitive psychology. She differentiated between two validation foci: *construct representation* refers to the cognitive or processing mechanisms that generate task performance, while *nomothetic span* refers to the network of correlational relationships among measures. Embretson argued that cognitive psychology had over-emphasized nomothetic span at the expense of construct representation—focusing on whether tasks correlate appropriately with each other rather than on what cognitive processes actually produce performance. As she noted, "internal construct validity—representing the construct—is just as important as external construct validity—relating to other measures" (p. 179). This distinction foreshadows our central argument: correlational patterns (nomothetic span) cannot substitute for explicit models of cognitive processes (construct representation).

The causal perspective on validity: Our theoretical foundation. @Borsboom2004 sharpened these ideas by defining validity in explicitly causal terms: "A test is valid for measuring an attribute if and only if (a) the attribute exists and (b) variations in the attribute causally produce variations in the measurement outcomes" (p. 1061). This definition makes clear that validity is not merely about correlational patterns or predictive utility—it requires that the construct actually *causes* the observed behavior. This causal requirement cannot be verified through correlations alone, no matter how extensive the nomological network. It demands theoretical specification of the causal mechanisms linking constructs to observations.

This manuscript operationalizes Borsboom's framework through computational cognitive models. When we specify cognitive constructs as process parameters in generative models, we make explicit claims about (a) what cognitive attributes exist (as formalized process mechanisms) and (b) how variations in those attributes causally produce measurement outcomes (through specified computational operations). Parameter recovery analysis then provides empirical tests of the causal link: do individual differences in behavioral indicators actually track individual differences in the cognitive processes that generated them? This approach moves Borsboom's causal conception from philosophical principle to practical methodology.

Convergent perspective: Relational definitions via generative models. Recent work by @KvamAlaukik2025 converges on this approach from a complementary direction. They argue for moving beyond operational definitions (fixed procedures tied to single indicators) toward "relational definitions" via multilevel generative models. Constructs are defined through their relationships across multiple measurements, captured by latent variables in hierarchical models. Crucially, they show that model-based metrics have superior measurement properties (reliability, validity) compared to behavioral scores precisely because generative models specify how cognitive processes produce observations. Our framework aligns with and extends this argument: relational definitions require explicit generative mechanisms, and parameter recovery provides the empirical foundation for assessing whether indicators validly capture those relationally-defined constructs.

Why classical frameworks demand more than they deliver. These frameworks consistently emphasize theoretical commitments: nomological networks, trait-method distinctions, score meaning, construct representation, and causal mechanisms. However, in much cognitive task research, these theoretical requirements remain underspecified across domains. What exactly is the "attention control" construct that conflict tasks measure? What cognitive processes constitute "working memory capacity"? How do individual differences in "processing speed" parameters causally generate differences in reaction times? Without explicit answers, validation efforts rely heavily on convergent validity—the expectation that tasks assumed to measure the same construct should correlate. But as we show next, this logic becomes circular when the construct itself is defined primarily through task correlations, and this circularity problem is universal across cognitive measurement domains.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## The Circularity Problem in Task-Based Validation

Individual-differences research in cognitive psychology has long grappled with persistent empirical patterns that challenge simple interpretations of what cognitive tasks measure. These patterns are particularly evident in research on executive functions and attention control, but they extend broadly across cognitive domains [@HedgeEtAl2018]. Understanding these phenomena—and why they resist resolution through improved psychometric methods—reveals fundamental limits of correlation-based validation approaches.

The empirical landscape. Decades of research reveal three interrelated patterns. First, tasks presumed to measure the same cognitive construct often show weak cross-task correlations, typically ranging from *r* = .0 to .4, even when individual tasks show adequate reliability [@HedgeEtAl2018; @ReyMermetEtAl2018; @ReyMermetEtAl2019; @EnkaviEtAl2019]. Second, task-specific effects dominate: the majority of reliable variance in cognitive task performance cannot be explained by latent factors corresponding to theorized constructs [@EnkaviEtAl2019; @ReyMermetEtAl2019]. Third, different scoring methods from the same task paradigm can show dramatically different psychometric properties and relationships to other measures—a pattern termed the "reliability paradox" [@HedgeEtAl2018]. Mean performance scores typically show high reliability but low convergent validity, while difference scores often show the opposite pattern.

These phenomena are not subtle or occasional—they are systematic and robust across task domains, scoring methods, and samples. They persist despite substantial sample sizes, careful experimental control, and sophisticated psychometric modeling. Most critically for present purposes, they persist even when researchers employ state-of-the-art psychometric practices.

The toolbox approach as sophisticated response: Attention control. @DraheimEtAl2021 provide an exemplary case study of how contemporary psychometric sophistication addresses these challenges in attention research. Recognizing that simple difference scores can be unreliable, they systematically compared alternative scoring methods (e.g., delta scores, proportional scores) and selected those maximizing reliability. They employed a "toolbox" sampling strategy, including multiple variants of conflict tasks (Flanker, Simon, Stroop families) to capture the breadth of attention control mechanisms. Through confirmatory factor analysis, they demonstrated that these optimized indicators loaded on coherent factors representing attention control constructs.

This work represents genuine methodological advance. The psychometric rigor is exemplary, the measurement models are sophisticated, and the factor structures appear theoretically sensible. Yet even after these careful psychometric interventions, fundamental questions remain: Does factor coherence demonstrate that tasks measure shared cognitive processes? Do optimized difference scores capture the intended control mechanisms? When tasks *don't* correlate strongly, is this because they measure distinct processes, or because our indicators inadequately capture shared processes?

Parallel challenges in working memory research. The same pattern emerges in working memory capacity (WMC) measurement. @WilhelmEtAl2013 posed the foundational question: "What is WMC?"—observing that span tasks, N-back tasks, and updating tasks all purport to measure working memory, yet show inconsistent correlational patterns. @BurgoyneEtAl2024 documented that complex span and N-back tasks correlate only moderately (*r* ≈ .30-.40), despite both being treated as "WMC measures" and showing adequate internal reliability. @WilhelmEtAl2025 demonstrated through computational modeling that different WM tasks fractionate into distinct subfunctions—storage, processing, and updating—raising the question: Which process (if any) constitutes "the" WMC construct? As in attention research, sophisticated factor analysis can extract shared variance, but cannot determine what cognitive mechanisms produce that covariance. Does the latent factor represent storage capacity, executive attention, or efficient binding mechanisms? The correlation patterns alone cannot answer.

Processing speed as theoretical construct. Processing speed research faces similar ambiguities beneath apparent measurement success. @Donders1868 proposed decomposing reaction time into stages (encoding, decision, motor execution), assuming that RT differences across tasks reflect selective manipulation of target stages. However, @SchmitzWilhelm2016 showed that distributional parameters of RT (ex-Gaussian τ, reflecting slow tail responses) relate differently to external criteria than mean RT, suggesting that "processing speed" conflates multiple distinct temporal processes. When simple RT, choice RT, and Stroop RT all load on a "speed factor," does this indicate a unitary speed mechanism, or shared variance from encoding efficiency, motor execution speed, and strategic cautiousness? Again, factor coherence does not resolve what processes generate the shared variance.

Why convergent validity cannot adjudicate. The core problem becomes apparent when we trace the validation logic, and this circularity is universal across cognitive measurement domains. The reasoning chain is identical whether applied to attention, working memory, or processing speed:

*Attention example:* (1) We theorize that Flanker and Simon tasks both require attention control. (2) We observe that these tasks correlate. (3) We conclude that this correlation validates attention control as a construct and confirms that both tasks measure it. But why did we predict a correlation in step 1? Because we assumed both tasks measure attention control.

*Working memory example:* (1) We theorize that span tasks and N-back tasks both require WMC. (2) We observe that these tasks correlate (albeit modestly, *r* ≈ .30-.40). (3) We conclude that this validates WMC and confirms both tasks measure it. But the correlation was predicted by the assumption it purports to validate.

*Processing speed example:* (1) We theorize that simple RT and choice RT both require processing speed. (2) We observe correlation. (3) We conclude this validates "speed" as a construct. But we predicted correlation by assuming both tasks measure speed.

As @Borsboom2004 noted, this creates "a closed circle of test interpretation where tests are validated by their intercorrelations, and the intercorrelations are explained by appealing to the constructs that were posited to explain those intercorrelations in the first place" (p. 1066).

The circularity is not resolved by factor analysis, regardless of domain. Extracting a latent factor shows that tasks share variance, but the psychological meaning of that factor—what cognitive mechanisms create the shared variance—is inferred from theoretical assumptions about what the tasks measure. The "attention control" factor might reflect controlled processing, but might equally reflect general processing speed or motivation. The "WMC" factor might reflect storage capacity, but might equally reflect executive attention or processing efficiency. The "speed" factor might reflect neural transmission speed, but might equally reflect motor execution speed or strategic cautiousness. Without independent specification of what processes generate the factor, interpreting it with construct labels merely relabels the correlation pattern with theoretical terminology.

Why this is not a failure of individual research programs. It is critical to understand that this circularity is not a shortcoming of @DraheimEtAl2021, @WilhelmEtAl2013, or similar careful psychometric work. Their approaches exemplify current best practices in their respective domains—arguably the strongest empirical foundations one can build using correlation-based methods. The problem is not that they executed poorly, but that the validation logic itself has fundamental limitations that apply equally across all cognitive measurement domains. As @Whitely1983 recognized, nomothetic span (correlational patterns among tasks) cannot substitute for construct representation (models of how cognitive processes generate performance). No matter how sophisticated the psychometric modeling, correlations alone cannot determine what processes produce them.

This becomes especially clear when we consider what happens when tasks *don't* correlate as expected. @OberauerEtAl2024 found that latent factors extracted from attention control tasks showed stronger relationships with processing speed than with working memory capacity—contrary to theoretical predictions. @UnsworthEngle2007 found that span task performance correlated with controlled attention measures, but the underlying mechanisms (capacity limits vs. attentional control) remained ambiguous. @SchmitzWilhelm2016 found that RT distributional parameters related differently to ability measures than mean RT, questioning which "speed" construct those correlations validated. In each case: Does this invalidate the construct, or merely indicate that current indicators inadequately measure it? The correlation patterns cannot answer this question because the theoretical interpretation remains underdetermined by the data.

The theoretical solution. Breaking this circularity requires independent specification of constructs—theoretical models that define cognitive processes and predict their behavioral consequences without presupposing the correlational patterns those predictions aim to explain. As @CronbachMeehl1955 recognized, validation requires that "the network makes contact with observations independent of the test" (p. 291). In cognitive psychology, this means explicit models of how hypothesized processes generate behavioral data. Such models enable validation through parameter recovery: if a model accurately captures how a process operates, and if a behavioral indicator reflects that process, then individual differences in the indicator should recover individual differences in the model parameter. This logic does not rely on correlations among tasks—it relies on the explicit theoretical claim that a process causally generates an observable pattern.

The following sections develop this model-based framework systematically. Section 4 reconceptualizes cognitive constructs as processes rather than latent factors, clarifying what "construct representation" means. Section 5 examines different levels of theoretical formalization, from verbal theories to generative models, identifying what each level can establish about validity. Section 6 addresses the concern that model-dependence makes validity arbitrary, arguing instead that model-relativity represents epistemic progress. Section 7 details the parameter recovery logic that enables validity testing through generative models.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## From Constructs to Processes

Reframing Validity

Breaking the circularity of correlation-based validation requires reconceptualizing cognitive constructs themselves. Rather than treating constructs as latent variables that cause correlations among tasks, we propose understanding them as hypothesized cognitive *processes*—mechanisms that causally generate observable behavior. This shift is not merely semantic; it fundamentally changes how validity is assessed and what evidence can establish it.

Traditional construct conceptualization. In much individual-differences research, cognitive constructs are treated as latent factors—unobserved variables that account for shared variance among observable indicators. "Attention control" or "working memory capacity" are modeled as latent factors causing correlations among tasks presumed to require these abilities. The construct's existence is inferred from correlation patterns, and its interpretation depends on theoretical assumptions about what the correlated tasks measure. This approach naturally leads to validation through convergent validity: if tasks correlate appropriately with theoretically expected tasks and inappropriately with theoretically unexpected tasks, the construct and its measurement are considered validated.

Process-based reconceptualization. A process-oriented view begins from a different starting point. Cognitive constructs are not simply latent variables that cause correlations—they are hypotheses about information-processing mechanisms that generate behavior. "Attention control" refers not to a source of covariance, but to specific cognitive mechanisms: resolving conflict between competing responses, suppressing irrelevant information, shielding working memory from distraction, or flexibly adjusting processing strategies. These mechanisms have computational and temporal properties. They operate on representations, produce observable consequences, and can be specified with varying degrees of formality.

Critically, when constructs are understood as processes, tasks do not "measure" constructs directly—tasks create conditions under which processes operate and produce observable outcomes. A Flanker task does not "measure attention control" in the way a thermometer measures temperature. Instead, it creates a situation where certain control processes (e.g., resolving response conflict) contribute to determining reaction times and error rates. The extent to which performance indexes a specific control process depends on the architecture of processing—what processes are engaged, how they interact, and how their activity maps onto observable behavior.

Validity as process-observation mapping. This reconceptualization changes what validity means. A task indicator is valid for a cognitive process to the extent that individual differences in that process causally influence individual differences in the indicator, under explicit theoretical assumptions about how processing unfolds. Validity becomes a property of the theoretical model linking processes to observations, not a property of tasks or correlations.

This definition has several important implications. First, validity is *graded*: an indicator can be more or less valid depending on how strongly and selectively the target process influences it. Second, validity is *parameter-specific*: different indicators from the same task may be differentially valid for different process parameters. Third, validity is *model-relative*: claims about indicator validity depend on theoretical commitments about processing architecture. Fourth, validity is *testable*: if a process model is sufficiently specified, we can derive predictions about which indicators should be sensitive to which processes, and these predictions can be evaluated against data.

Multiple levels of description. Understanding constructs as processes does not require reducing all psychological explanation to neural mechanisms. Following @Marr1982, cognitive theories can specify processes at multiple levels: computational (what problems are solved), algorithmic (what procedures solve them), and implementational (how neural systems realize those procedures). Process theories in cognitive psychology typically operate at computational and algorithmic levels, specifying what information is processed and what operations transform it. For example, conflict resolution might be described computationally as weighted integration of competing evidence streams, or algorithmically as mutual inhibition between response representations with temporally evolving activation. These descriptions can have psychological utility even without complete neural specification.

Connection to causal theories of validity. This process-oriented framework aligns closely with @Borsboom2004's causal perspective on validity. They argued that validity requires (a) the attribute exists and (b) variations in it causally produce variations in measurement outcomes. When we specify cognitive constructs as processes, we make explicit claims about what exists (specific information-processing mechanisms) and how they generate data (through specified computational/algorithmic operations). Validity then becomes assessable by evaluating whether the hypothesized causal mechanisms actually operate as theorized—whether individual differences in process parameters do, in fact, causally produce individual differences in indicators.

From constructs to parameters. When processes are formalized in computational models, individual differences in cognitive constructs correspond to individual differences in model parameters. A parameter represents a theoretically meaningful property of a process: the rate of evidence accumulation, the threshold for response execution, the decay rate of working memory representations, or the efficiency of conflict resolution. If the model adequately captures how processing unfolds, then individual differences in these parameters represent individual differences in the cognitive processes of interest. This grounds construct measurement in explicit theoretical commitments about psychological mechanisms rather than in correlations among tasks.

Implications for measurement. If constructs are processes and individual differences reside in process parameters, then valid measurement requires indicators that track parameter variation. This cannot be determined from correlational patterns alone—it requires understanding how the process architecture generates observable data. As we develop in subsequent sections, this understanding can range from qualitative process theories with testable implications, to semi-formal frameworks that specify key constraints, to fully generative models that simulate the complete data-generating process. Each level of formalization enables different kinds of validity assessment, but all share the requirement that validity claims must be grounded in explicit theories of how processes generate observations.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Graded Formalization

From Verbal Theories to Generative Models

The requirement that validity claims rest on explicit process theories raises an immediate practical question: how formal must process theories be? Must every validity claim be grounded in complete computational models, or can less formal theoretical frameworks suffice? We argue for a graded view of formalization, where different levels of theoretical precision enable different strengths of validity claims. Understanding this continuum clarifies both what current practice can achieve and where generative models provide distinctive advantages.

The formalization continuum. Process theories vary in their degree of formalization—the extent to which they specify mechanisms precisely enough to generate quantitative predictions. At one extreme are purely verbal theories that describe processes qualitatively (e.g., "attention control enables task-relevant processing and suppresses task-irrelevant information"). At the other extreme are complete generative models that specify all processes and parameters sufficiently to simulate behavioral data trial-by-trial. Between these extremes lie semi-formal frameworks that identify component processes and specify key relationships without complete quantitative specification.

This is not a dichotomy between "good" (formal) and "bad" (informal) theories. Rather, it reflects different stages of theoretical development and different research goals. Verbal theories can generate important qualitative insights and motivate experimental research. Semi-formal frameworks can test process decompositions and identify when simple task-process mappings fail. Fully formal models can assess parameter identifiability and quantify indicator validity. Each level of formalization enables certain kinds of inferences while remaining limited in others.

Verbal process theories. Descriptive theoretical frameworks specify cognitive processes and their functional roles without mathematical formalization. For example, @FriedmanMiyake2004 conceptualized executive functions as involving separable processes of inhibition, updating, and shifting. This framework guided experimental design and measurement decisions, but the verbal descriptions alone could not predict precisely how these processes would manifest in specific behavioral indicators. Such frameworks are valuable for organizing research programs and generating hypotheses, but they cannot fully specify the process-observation link necessary for strong validity claims.

Semi-formal process frameworks. More structured approaches decompose tasks into component processes and systematically test predictions about each component. @FrischkornOberauer2025 provide a compelling example with the antisaccade task. Rather than assuming the task measures a unitary "inhibition" construct, they decomposed performance into three theoretically distinct components: (1) orienting toward the cue, (2) selecting and executing the antisaccade response, and (3) maintaining task goals in working memory. Through systematic experimental manipulations—varying cue strength, response requirements, and memory demands—they demonstrated that each component makes separable contributions to antisaccade performance and shows distinct relationships to other measures.

Similar process decomposition approaches have proven valuable across cognitive domains. @UnsworthEngle2007 decomposed working memory capacity into primary memory (active maintenance) and secondary memory (retrieval) components, showing through systematic manipulation that different WM tasks emphasize these processes differently—explaining weak span-N-back correlations as differential process emphasis rather than construct distinctiveness. In processing speed research, @SchmitzWilhelm2016 decomposed RT distributions into theoretically meaningful parameters (location, scale, shape), demonstrating that different distributional aspects relate differently to intelligence and development—challenging the assumption that "processing speed" is unitary.

This semi-formal approach enables stronger validity claims than purely verbal theories. By specifying component processes and deriving testable predictions about how experimental manipulations should affect each component, the framework allows falsification of simple task-process mappings. When @FrischkornOberauer2025 showed that multiple distinct processes contribute to antisaccade performance, they demonstrated that treating antisaccades as measuring a unitary "inhibition" construct oversimplifies the actual processing architecture. This represents genuine theoretical progress—it constrains interpretations of performance based on explicit process theory and systematic evidence—and the principle applies broadly: attention control, working memory, and processing speed all benefit from process decomposition over unitary construct assumptions.

However, semi-formal approaches face inherent limitations for certain validity questions. They can establish that multiple processes contribute to task performance, but they cannot precisely quantify how much each process contributes to any given indicator. They can show that experimental manipulations affect performance through predicted pathways, but they cannot determine whether individual differences in behavioral indicators selectively reflect individual differences in specific processes versus confounds of multiple processes. These limitations become critical when the goal is to use tasks as measures of individual differences in specific cognitive mechanisms.

Computational process models. Formal computational models specify processes mathematically, using equations or algorithms that describe how information is processed and transformed. For example, the Diffusion Model for Conflict tasks [@UlrichEtAl2015] specifies how controlled and automatic processes contribute to response selection through equations describing evidence accumulation, with parameters representing drift rates, decision thresholds, and temporal dynamics. These models make precise quantitative predictions about reaction time distributions and error rates that can be tested against data.

Formal models enable stronger validity claims than semi-formal frameworks in several ways. First, they make all assumptions explicit—every process, parameter, and relationship is specified precisely, leaving no hidden degrees of freedom. Second, they reveal identifiability constraints—whether proposed parameters can be uniquely estimated from data, or whether multiple parameter combinations produce equivalent predictions. Third, they enable parameter estimation—fitting models to data provides quantitative estimates of each individual's process parameters, which can then be related to other measures or outcomes.

Generative models and parameter recovery. The strongest validity assessments come from *generative* models—models sufficiently specified to simulate complete datasets. Generative models enable a distinctive validation approach: parameter recovery analysis. By simulating data from known parameter values, computing behavioral indicators from simulated data, and testing whether indicators successfully recover the generating parameters, researchers can directly assess which indicators validly measure which process parameters under the model's assumptions.

This breaks the circularity of correlation-based validation because the validation criterion—the generating parameter values—is independent of the tasks and indicators being validated. We don't assume indicators are valid and then check if they correlate appropriately; we specify a process theory, generate data from it, and empirically test whether indicators track the processes that generated the data. This provides strong evidence about indicator validity specifically in the context of that theoretical framework.

When is formalization necessary? Different research questions require different levels of theoretical formalization. For exploratory research identifying relevant cognitive processes, verbal or semi-formal frameworks may suffice. For testing whether experimental manipulations affect specific process components, semi-formal frameworks with systematic experimentation can be powerful. For assessing whether individual differences in behavioral indicators reflect individual differences in specific cognitive processes—the core question for construct validity in individual differences research—generative models provide the most rigorous approach.

Importantly, requiring formal models for strong individual-differences validity claims does not dismiss decades of research based on less formal approaches. Rather, it clarifies the logical limits of what different types of evidence can establish. Correlational patterns and factor structures contribute valuable information about nomothetic networks, but they cannot establish construct representation. Semi-formal frameworks can falsify oversimplified task-process mappings, but they cannot precisely quantify parameter-indicator relationships. Each approach has value, and each has limits. Understanding these limits helps researchers choose appropriate methods for their specific validity questions.

The antisaccade case revisited. The @FrischkornOberauer2025 antisaccade work illustrates both the value and the limits of semi-formal approaches. Their process decomposition and systematic experimentation provide compelling evidence that antisaccades cannot be interpreted as measuring unitary inhibition. This substantially advances theoretical understanding and appropriately constrains interpretation of antisaccade-based individual differences. However, their approach cannot definitively answer certain questions: Do difference scores versus mean scores differentially reflect orienting versus response selection versus maintenance? How much do individual differences in each component contribute to commonly used antisaccade indicators? Would alternative process decompositions fit the data equally well?

These questions are not criticisms of their excellent work—they simply illustrate what semi-formal approaches cannot resolve. Answering such questions would require full generative models that specify how each process component quantitatively contributes to trial-level behavior. This is not necessary for all research purposes, but it becomes necessary when the goal is to ground individual differences measurement in specific process mechanisms. The next sections develop this argument by showing how generative models enable precise validity assessment through parameter recovery analysis.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Model-Relativity as Feature, Not Bug

The dependence of validity claims on specific theoretical models raises an immediate concern: if validity is relative to model assumptions, doesn't this make validity arbitrary? If different models might yield different validity conclusions, how can we trust any model-based validity claim? This concern must be addressed directly, because model-relativity is indeed a central feature of the framework we propose. However, rather than undermining validity claims, making them model-relative actually represents epistemic progress.

The objection articulated. The worry proceeds as follows: Suppose two different cognitive models—say, the Diffusion Model for Conflict (DMC) and the Shrinking Spotlight model (SSP)—both provide adequate fits to conflict task data but make somewhat different assumptions about processing architecture. Parameter recovery analyses might indicate that different behavioral indicators optimally measure different parameters under each model. If validity depends on which model we assume, and if models are themselves underdetermined by data, haven't we simply replaced one arbitrary choice (which indicators to use) with another (which model to believe)? This seems to leave validity claims fundamentally uncertain.

Model-relativity versus measurement nihilism. The key insight is that model-relativity is not equivalent to arbitrariness or nihilism. When we say validity is model-relative, we mean that statements about what indicators measure depend on theoretical commitments about how cognition works—specifically, about what processes operate and how they generate observable behavior. This is not a limitation unique to model-based approaches; it is a fundamental logical requirement for any validity claim. The alternative to model-relative validity is not model-independent validity—it is validity claims based on *implicit* or *unexamined* theoretical assumptions.

Consider common interpretive practices across domains. Interpreting Flanker effect sizes (RT incongruent - RT congruent) as measuring "conflict resolution ability" assumes that conflict creates competition between responses, that resolving conflict requires selective attention, and that other processes affect both conditions equally. Interpreting span task scores as measuring "working memory capacity" assumes that the storage-plus-processing demands specifically tax capacity limits rather than attention control, retrieval strategies, or processing efficiency. Interpreting mean RT as measuring "processing speed" assumes that RT primarily reflects speed of cognitive operations rather than response caution, motor execution time, or strategic time-accuracy trade-offs. These interpretations all rest on implicit process models that specify what cognitive operations occur and how they generate observations. The models are no less present for being informal—they simply haven't been made explicit or tested.

Making models explicit through formalization doesn't create model-dependence—it makes pre-existing theoretical commitments visible and testable. This is progress because explicit models can be evaluated, compared, and refined in ways that implicit assumptions cannot.

How model-relativity enables progress. Far from undermining validity claims, model-relativity provides the foundation for theoretical progress through several mechanisms:

First, explicit models can be tested. Once a process theory is formalized, its predictions can be systematically evaluated against data. Does the model generate the qualitative patterns observed in behavior? Can it account for effects of experimental manipulations? Does it predict novel phenomena? Models can fail these tests in ways that informal theories cannot, because informal theories lack sufficient precision to generate falsifiable predictions. The testability of formal models means that poor models can be identified and rejected.

Second, competing models generate discriminating evidence. When different models make different predictions about behavioral patterns, experimental or analytical work can distinguish them. @KvamEtAl2024 demonstrated this approach by systematically comparing how different evidence accumulation models account for patterns in perceptual decision-making. Similarly, @DonkinEtAl2015 showed how careful model comparison can reveal which processing assumptions are necessary to account for observed data. Model comparison is not always definitive—models may make similar predictions in some contexts while differing in others—but it provides a principled path toward identifying which theoretical commitments are better supported.

Third, model refinement improves understanding. When models fail to account for observed patterns, they can often be refined to address their limitations. The history of evidence accumulation modeling illustrates this: simpler models like the basic diffusion model have been extended to incorporate attention, confidence, multi-alternative choice, and other phenomena they initially couldn't explain. Each refinement involves explicit theoretical commitments that can be evaluated. This iterative process of model testing and refinement represents scientific progress in understanding cognitive mechanisms.

Validity claims are always conditional. Recognizing model-relativity clarifies that all validity claims are conditional: "*Given* theoretical assumptions X about processing architecture, behavioral indicator Y validly measures process parameter Z with strength W." This conditionality is not a weakness—it is epistemic honesty. The alternative is to make validity claims that sound unconditional ("Flanker effects measure attention control") but actually depend on implicit theoretical assumptions that remain unexamined and untested.

Conditional validity claims are scientifically useful. They establish what follows *if* certain theoretical commitments hold, which can be independently evaluated. They make clear what evidence would challenge the validity claim (either empirical evidence against the indicator-parameter relationship, or theoretical/empirical evidence against the processing model). They enable cumulative theoretical progress as models are tested, compared, and refined.

Practical implications. Accepting model-relativity has several practical implications for measurement. First, researchers must justify their choice of processing model theoretically and evaluate its adequacy empirically. This might involve testing model predictions against qualitative patterns, comparing competing models, or assessing model identifiability and parameter recovery. Second, validity claims should be explicit about their conditional nature—what model assumptions they depend on and what evidence would challenge them. Third, when strong individual-differences claims are at stake, investing in formal model development and testing becomes more worthwhile than simply accumulating tasks and correlations.

Importantly, this doesn't mean that every research project requires building new computational models. Existing well-tested models can support validity claims when they adequately capture relevant processes. Semi-formal approaches can constrain interpretations even when complete models aren't available. Purely exploratory research can proceed with weaker validity commitments while recognizing their limitations. The key is matching the strength of validity claims to the strength of theoretical justification.

Why some models are better than others. Model-relativity does not imply that all models are equally good or that model choice is arbitrary. Models can be better or worse along multiple dimensions: empirical adequacy (fit to data), theoretical coherence (consistency with established principles), identifiability (whether parameters can be uniquely estimated), parsimony (complexity relative to explanatory power), and generalizability (applicability across contexts). These criteria provide principled bases for model evaluation and comparison. While model selection may sometimes remain uncertain—multiple models may fit data comparably—this uncertainty honestly reflects the current state of theoretical knowledge rather than hiding it behind seemingly objective but actually assumption-laden correlational analyses.

The claim is not that formal models eliminate uncertainty or arbitrariness in validity assessment. Rather, formal models make the sources of uncertainty explicit, testable, and subject to empirical constraint. This is the epistemic advantage of model-based validity: it trades hidden assumptions for explicit theoretical commitments that can be evaluated, challenged, and improved.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Generative Models and Parameter Recovery Logic

Having established that validity requires explicit process theories and that model-relativity represents epistemic progress rather than nihilism, we now turn to the specific advantages of generative cognitive models for validity assessment. Generative models—computational models sufficiently specified to simulate complete behavioral datasets—enable a distinctive approach to validity testing through parameter recovery analysis. This section explains the logic of this approach and why it provides stronger validity evidence than correlation-based methods.

What are generative models? A generative model is a computational model that specifies all processes and parameters necessary to simulate behavioral data trial-by-trial. For example, a generative model of decision-making in a Flanker task would specify: how perceptual evidence is encoded, how automatic and controlled processes contribute to response selection, how evidence accumulates over time, when responses are initiated, and how noise affects each stage. Given values for all model parameters, the model can generate synthetic behavioral data (reaction times and accuracy) that match the structure of real experimental data.

The key feature of generative models is completeness: they don't just describe processes qualitatively or fit aggregate patterns—they specify the entire data-generating process. This completeness enables a unique form of validity testing that other approaches cannot provide.

Parameter recovery logic. The fundamental logic of parameter recovery analysis is straightforward: if a model correctly describes how cognitive processes generate behavior, and if a behavioral indicator reflects a specific process, then individual differences in the indicator should track individual differences in the corresponding model parameter. Conversely, if an indicator confounds multiple processes or is insensitive to the target process, parameter recovery will be weak even if the model is correct.

The method proceeds in four steps:

1.  Specify the generative model. Choose a computational model that formalizes theory about how processes operate. For example, the Diffusion Model for Conflict (DMC; @UlrichEtAl2015) specifies how automatic activation and controlled processing jointly determine response selection in conflict tasks.

2.  Simulate data with known parameters. Generate synthetic behavioral data from the model, where the true parameter values are known because we set them. Critically, we introduce individual differences by varying parameter values across simulated participants, mimicking how real individuals might differ in their cognitive processes.

3.  Compute behavioral indicators. From the simulated data, calculate the same behavioral indicators that researchers typically compute from real data—mean reaction times, accuracy rates, difference scores, etc. These indicators are computed exactly as they would be in empirical research, without knowledge of the generating parameters.

4.  Assess parameter recovery. Correlate the behavioral indicators with the generating parameter values. Strong correlations indicate that the indicator successfully tracks individual differences in the parameter—that it validly measures the cognitive process that parameter represents, given the model's assumptions. Weak correlations indicate that the indicator either confounds multiple processes or fails to capture the target process.

Why this breaks circularity. Parameter recovery analysis provides an independent validity criterion that correlation-based approaches lack. When we assess validity through cross-task correlations, we use assumptions about what tasks measure to predict correlations, then interpret observed correlations as validating those assumptions. Parameter recovery avoids this circularity because the criterion—the generating parameter values—is established independently of any assumptions about task validity. We don't assume indicators are valid and check if they correlate appropriately; we specify a process theory, generate data from it, and empirically test whether indicators track the processes that generated the data.

This doesn't eliminate theoretical assumptions—the validity claims remain conditional on the correctness of the process model. But it changes the nature of the assumptions: rather than assuming tasks measure constructs and using correlations to validate that assumption, we assume a model of how processes work and test whether indicators empirically track those processes. The model's adequacy can be evaluated independently through standard model-fitting and model comparison techniques.

What parameter recovery reveals. Parameter recovery analyses can reveal several important patterns relevant to validity assessment:

Differential validity across indicators. Different behavioral indicators from the same task may show very different recovery patterns. For example, reaction time indicators might strongly recover parameters related to processing speed, while accuracy indicators might recover parameters related to decision caution. This shows that "the task" doesn't have a single validity—different indicators have different validities for different constructs.

Parameter confounding in indicators. An indicator might correlate with multiple model parameters because it reflects an aggregate of multiple processes. This reveals that the indicator is not process-pure, even if it shows high reliability and appropriate correlational patterns with other measures.

Reliability-validity dissociations. An indicator might show high split-half reliability but weak parameter recovery, indicating that it measures *something* consistently but not the intended process. Conversely, an indicator might show modest reliability but strong parameter recovery when the target process is measured with noise. This challenges the common assumption that reliability guarantees construct validity.

Identification of optimal indicators. By comparing recovery correlations across multiple candidate indicators, researchers can identify which indicators most validly measure which processes. This enables data-driven optimization of measurement while remaining grounded in explicit theory.

Comparison with intermediate models. Researchers can compare whether simplified models (e.g., fitting a basic diffusion model to conflict task data) better recover process parameters than raw behavioral indicators. This tests whether intermediate computational modeling improves validity or whether simpler behavioral measures suffice.

Connection to psychometric theory. Parameter recovery analysis shares conceptual similarities with validation approaches in psychometric measurement theory, particularly item response theory (IRT). In IRT, item parameters are estimated from response patterns, and item validity can be assessed by how well items discriminate between individuals with different trait levels. The key parallel is that both approaches evaluate measurement quality by testing how well indicators track underlying constructs—but parameter recovery does so through generative simulation rather than through correlational structure alone.

The computational psychometrics framework [@Vandekerckhove2011; @Vandekerckhove2014] extends these ideas to complex cognitive processes. Rather than treating cognitive abilities as unidimensional traits, it models the specific cognitive processes that generate task performance. Individual differences reside in process parameters rather than in factor scores. Validity assessment then focuses on whether behavioral indicators successfully capture those parameter-level differences.

Advantages over correlational validation. Parameter recovery provides several advantages for validity assessment:

*Independence from criterion measures.* No external criterion task is needed—the model's parameters serve as the validity criterion. This avoids the problem that criterion measures may themselves have questionable validity.

*Quantitative precision.* Recovery correlations provide quantitative estimates of indicator validity, enabling principled comparison of alternative indicators.

*Process specificity.* Recovery analyses can distinguish which aspects of a multifaceted process each indicator captures, rather than treating "the construct" as unitary.

*Theoretical transparency.* Because validity claims are explicitly conditional on the model, the theoretical assumptions underlying validity claims are clear and testable.

*Generalizability testing.* By simulating across different parameter ranges, sample sizes, or noise levels, researchers can assess when and how strongly validity claims hold.

*Cross-domain applicability.* The parameter recovery logic applies wherever generative models can be specified: conflict tasks (as demonstrated in Sections 10-12), memory tasks [@OberauerEtAl2018], decision-making [@DonkinEtAl2015], and perceptual discrimination [@KvamEtAl2024]. The specific models differ by domain, but the validity assessment framework remains constant.

Limitations and requirements. Parameter recovery analysis is not a panacea. It requires well-specified generative models, which demand substantial theoretical and technical investment. The validity conclusions are conditional on model adequacy—if the model misrepresents how processes operate, parameter recovery analyses may be misleading. Model testing and comparison remain necessary. Furthermore, strong parameter recovery in simulation doesn't guarantee that real behavioral data will support the same interpretations—real data contain complexities (strategic variation, learning, fatigue) that simulations may not capture.

Nevertheless, parameter recovery provides a principled framework for validity assessment that makes theoretical assumptions explicit and testable. When models adequately capture cognitive processes, parameter recovery offers strong evidence about which indicators validly measure which processes.

From principles to practice. Having established the theoretical foundations and methodological logic of model-based validity assessment, we now turn to concrete demonstrations. Sections 8-13 present systematic empirical analyses testing the framework's predictions and revealing patterns that clarify indicator meaning. We focus on conflict tasks—not because validity problems are unique to this domain, but because they exemplify challenges that appear broadly across cognitive measurement and because well-developed computational models enable rigorous testing.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Conflict Tasks and Competing Theoretical Models

Cognitive conflict tasks provide an ideal testbed for model-based validity analysis. These experimental paradigms—including Flanker, Simon, and Stroop tasks—are widely used to measure attention control, executive function, and related constructs. Conflict tasks create situations where competing sources of information (perceptual features, learned associations, or response tendencies) must be integrated to produce appropriate behavior. They include Flanker tasks (where target identity must be processed while ignoring distractors), Simon tasks (where spatial location of stimuli is irrelevant but automatically activates responses), and Stroop tasks (where word meaning must be ignored to report ink color).

Why conflict tasks? Conflict tasks are particularly appropriate for demonstrating model-based validity for several reasons. First, they are *ubiquitous* in cognitive and individual-differences research, used extensively to measure constructs such as attention control, inhibition, and cognitive flexibility [@DraheimEtAl2021; @FriedmanMiyake2004]. Second, they exhibit the key validity challenges identified in Section 3: weak cross-task correlations despite presumed shared constructs, reliability paradoxes, and task-specific effects [@HedgeEtAl2018; @ReyMermetEtAl2018]—challenges that parallel those in working memory (weak span-N-back correlations; @BurgoyneEtAl2024), processing speed (inconsistent RT-ability relationships; @SchmitzWilhelm2016), and cross-domain research (modest WMC-attention correlations; @OberauerEtAl2024). Third, they are supported by a rich *modeling tradition*—multiple computational models have been developed to explain conflict task performance, providing theoretical frameworks for validity analysis [@UlrichEtAl2015; @WhiteEtAl2011]. Finally, conflict tasks produce multiple potential behavioral indicators (reaction times in congruent and incongruent conditions, accuracy rates, various difference score formulations), raising questions about which indicators best capture which processes—questions that apply equally to indicator selection in span tasks, speed tasks, and other cognitive paradigms.

Standard interpretation and measurement practices. In typical individual-differences research, conflict tasks are interpreted as measuring a single construct—"attention control," "interference suppression," or "conflict resolution"—with task effects (e.g., RT incongruent minus RT congruent) treated as indicators of this construct. Larger effects are sometimes interpreted as worse control (more interference), smaller effects as better control (efficient suppression). Researchers aggregate across multiple conflict tasks and extract latent factors, interpreting these factors as measuring the shared attention control mechanism [@DraheimEtAl2021; @FriedmanMiyake2004].

However, conflict resolution is computationally complex. Resolving conflict requires detecting that conflict exists, adjusting processing strategies, selectively weighting relevant versus irrelevant information, and inhibiting prepotent but incorrect responses. These component processes may operate at different time scales and show distinct individual differences. Moreover, conflict task performance also reflects perceptual processing, response execution, decision criteria, and strategic adjustments—processes not directly related to conflict resolution per se. Which behavioral indicators best capture which aspects of this complex processing architecture?

Two computational models of conflict processing. To demonstrate model-based validity analysis, we focus on two well-established generative models that formalize different theoretical perspectives on conflict processing:

The Diffusion Model for Conflict (DMC; @UlrichEtAl2015) conceptualizes conflict processing as a race between automatic and controlled evidence accumulation. When a stimulus appears, two types of information contribute to response selection: *automatic activation* triggered rapidly by salient features (captured by amplitude parameter *A* and temporal dynamics parameter tau), and *controlled processing* that integrates task-relevant information more gradually (captured by controlled drift rate *mu_c*). These evidence streams combine to drive a diffusion process toward one of two response boundaries (separation *b*), with response execution delayed by non-decision time (*non_dec*). Individual differences in these parameters represent individual differences in specific aspects of conflict processing—automatic response capture versus controlled selection efficiency versus decision caution.

The Shrinking Spotlight model (SSP; @WhiteEtAl2011; @WhiteEtAl2018) takes an attentional focus perspective. Conflict arises because attention initially encompasses both task-relevant and task-irrelevant stimulus features. Over time, attention gradually narrows (shrinks) onto task-relevant features, reducing interference from irrelevant features. The model includes parameters for perceptual input strength (*p*), the initial spread of attentional focus (*sd_0*), boundary separation (*b*), and non-decision time (*non_dec*). Individual differences in spotlight shrinking dynamics represent individual differences in attentional selectivity.

Why two models? Using two models serves multiple purposes. First, it demonstrates that validity principles we identify are *architectural general*—they emerge from both models despite different processing assumptions. If patterns like parameter confounding or differential validity appeared only under DMC assumptions, they might be model-specific artifacts. Finding similar patterns under SSP's different architecture strengthens the conclusion that they reflect general constraints on behavioral measurement. Second, comparing models illustrates the model-relativity point from Section 6: different models make different predictions about which indicators measure which processes, precisely because they embody different theories about processing architecture. This clarifies that validity is conditional on theoretical commitments. Third, model comparison demonstrates the research practice we advocate: using multiple models to test generality of conclusions and clarify which results depend on specific theoretical assumptions.

Relationship between models. Despite their architectural differences, both DMC and SSP are generative accumulator models that produce trial-level predictions for reaction times and accuracy. Both include parameters for evidence accumulation, decision thresholds, and non-decision processes. However, they differ in how they represent conflict: DMC emphasizes the temporal dynamics of automatic versus controlled processing, while SSP emphasizes the spatial dynamics of attentional selection. These differences lead to somewhat different parameters of theoretical interest and somewhat different predictions about indicator validity—differences that illuminate how validity depends on processing theory.

What we do not claim. Our goal is neither to adjudicate between DMC and SSP, nor to argue that these models are the only or the best accounts of conflict processing. Other models exist [@WhiteEtAl2018; @SpaldingEtAl2022], and all current models surely represent simplified approximations to actual cognitive architecture. Our purpose is to demonstrate how generative models enable validity assessment by making process assumptions explicit and testable. The specific validity conclusions we draw are conditional on model assumptions, but the general patterns we identify—parameter confounding, differential validity, reliability-validity dissociations—appear robust across different models, suggesting they reflect fundamental constraints on behavioral measurement rather than model-specific artifacts.

Methodological approach. Our analyses employ parameter recovery simulations. We generate large synthetic datasets from each model, introducing realistic individual differences in process parameters. From these data we compute standard behavioral indicators (mean RT, mean accuracy, condition-specific scores, difference scores) as well as estimates from simplified models (the EZ-Diffusion Model; @WagenmakersEtAl2007). We then assess how strongly each indicator recovers each generating parameter through correlations, providing quantitative estimates of indicator validity under each model's assumptions. Details of simulation parameters, sample sizes, and analysis methods are provided in Section 9.

This approach allows us to systematically address validity questions that cannot be resolved through empirical studies alone: Which indicators selectively measure which processes? How does indicator validity depend on measurement properties like reliability? Do behavioral indicators outperform or underperform simplified computational models? How do validity patterns depend on theoretical assumptions about processing architecture? The following sections present simulation methods (Section 9) and key empirical findings (Sections 10-12) that demonstrate model-based validity analysis in practice.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Simulation Methodology

This section describes the parameter recovery simulations that form the empirical foundation for our validity analyses. We generated synthetic behavioral data from two cognitive models (DMC and SSP), computed standard behavioral indicators and simplified model estimates from these data, and assessed how well each indicator recovered the generating process parameters. This approach tests which indicators validly measure which processes under explicit theoretical assumptions.

Generative models. We conducted separate simulation studies using the Diffusion Model for Conflict (DMC; @UlrichEtAl2015) and the Shrinking Spotlight model (SSP; @WhiteEtAl2011). Both simulations followed identical design principles, differing only in the specific parameters and generating mechanisms. For DMC, we simulated individual differences in controlled drift rate (*mu_c*), automatic activation amplitude (*A*), temporal dynamics (*tau*), boundary separation (*b*), non-decision time (*non_dec*), and associated variability parameters. For SSP, we simulated individual differences in perceptual input (*p*), initial attentional spread (*sd_0*), boundary separation (*b*), and non-decision time (*non_dec*). Parameter values were drawn from distributions chosen to produce realistic behavioral patterns (RT ranges, accuracy levels, conflict effect magnitudes) comparable to empirical studies.

Design factors. We systematically varied two key design factors: (1) Sample size: N = 25, 50, 100, or 200 participants, and (2) Number of trials per condition: 50, 100, or 200 trials in each of two conditions (congruent/compatible and incongruent/incompatible). These factors allow us to examine how measurement precision (trial count) and statistical power (sample size) affect parameter recovery. Each design cell (combination of sample size and trial count) was replicated 250 times to obtain stable estimates of recovery patterns.

Data generation. For each simulated participant in each replication, we: (1) drew parameter values from specified distributions with realistic individual differences, (2) generated trial-level reaction times and accuracy using the model's generative process, and (3) output complete synthetic datasets structured identically to real experimental data. This process yielded over 60 million simulated trials across all conditions and replications.

Behavioral indicators. From the simulated behavioral data, we computed six standard indicators commonly used in individual-differences research: (1) Mean scores: average RT and accuracy across both conditions, (2) Condition-specific scores: RT and accuracy in the congruent/compatible condition only, (3) Incongruent/Incompatible scores: RT and accuracy in the incongruent/incompatible condition only, and (4) Difference scores: RT incongruent minus congruent, and accuracy differences. These indicators represent the primary measurement approaches used in attention control research.

Simplified model estimates. To test whether intermediate computational modeling improves validity over raw behavioral indicators, we also fit the EZ-Diffusion Model [@WagenmakersEtAl2007] to each simulated participant's data. The EZ-DM provides simplified estimates of drift rate (evidence accumulation speed), boundary separation (response caution), and non-decision time by fitting mean RT, RT variance, and accuracy.
