This tests whether a computationally simpler model that makes fewer architectural assumptions can better recover parameters from architecturally richer generative models (DMC or SSP).

Parameter recovery analysis. For each indicator and each generating parameter, we computed correlation coefficients quantifying how strongly the indicator tracked individual differences in the parameter. These recovery correlations serve as quantitative validity estimates: *r* = .70 indicates strong validity (70% shared variance between indicator and parameter), *r* = .40 indicates moderate validity, and *r* \< .20 indicates weak validity. We interpret absolute correlation values, as both positive and negative correlations indicate systematic relationships between parameters and indicators. We also computed reliability-corrected correlations to separate validity from measurement error.

Reliability estimation. To assess indicator reliability independently of parameter recovery, we computed split-half reliability using random splits of trials within each condition. For each simulated participant, we divided trials randomly into two halves, computed indicators from each half, and correlated them across participants. This provides estimates of indicator consistency that can be compared to parameter recovery.

Cross-task correlation simulations. In addition to parameter recovery, we conducted simulations testing whether parameter-level correlations transfer to indicator-level correlations. We generated data from two independent "tasks" (separate generative processes with the same model architecture) and induced correlations between corresponding parameters across tasks (e.g., controlled drift in Task 1 correlated with controlled drift in Task 2). We then tested whether these parameter correlations produced comparable correlations between behavioral indicators across tasks. This tests whether convergent validity at the behavioral level accurately reflects convergent validity at the process level.

Analysis approach. We focus primarily on patterns across conditions rather than precise numerical values in any single condition. Our goal is to identify systematic relationships—which indicators consistently recover which parameters, how these patterns differ across indicator types (RT vs accuracy, mean vs difference), and how they compare across models (DMC vs SSP). We also examine whether patterns depend on measurement properties (reliability) or design features (sample size, trial count). The robustness of patterns across conditions provides confidence that findings reflect general principles rather than specific parameter choices.

Presentation strategy. Main text analyses focus on DMC simulations, with four key figures illustrating central findings: selective parameter recovery and equifinality (comprehensive heatmap), reliability-validity dissociations, correlation transfer failures, and intermediate modeling comparisons. Complete parallel analyses for SSP are provided as supplementary materials, demonstrating that key findings generalize across model architectures. This presentation strategy balances conceptual clarity (avoiding redundancy) with empirical thoroughness (establishing generality).

Software and reproducibility. All simulations were conducted in R using custom functions for data generation, indicator computation, and parameter recovery analysis. The complete simulation code, generated datasets, and analysis scripts are available as part of the ComputationalValidity R package, ensuring full reproducibility of all reported findings. The package also includes functions allowing researchers to conduct similar parameter recovery analyses for their own measurement contexts.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Model-Based Validity Analysis

Key Findings

Parameter recovery simulations reveal three systematic patterns that clarify how behavioral indicators relate to underlying cognitive processes. Each pattern challenges common measurement assumptions and demonstrates why explicit process models are necessary for validity assessment. We present findings from DMC simulations here, with parallel SSP results confirming generalizability across model architectures (see Supplementary Materials).

### Equifinality and Selective Parameter Recovery

![Comprehensive parameter recovery matrix showing correlations between all DMC parameters and behavioral indicators. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals. The heatmap reveals selective parameter recovery: most indicators conflate multiple processes (process impurity), but RT difference scores achieve relatively selective recovery of control-related parameters.](../figures/manuscript/DMC_Equifinality.png)

Figure 1 presents a comprehensive analysis of how behavioral indicators recover underlying process parameters in the DMC. The 5×4 heatmap shows recovery correlations between all five DMC parameters (controlled drift μ~c~, temporal dynamics τ, automatic activation amplitude *A*, boundary separation *b*, and non-decision time *T*~er~) and four indicator types (RT difference, accuracy difference, RT mean, accuracy mean). Three patterns emerge that fundamentally constrain validity assessment.

Process impurity is the norm. Most behavioral indicators conflate multiple distinct cognitive processes, exhibiting what we term *process impurity*. Accuracy difference scores, for example, substantially recover both automatic activation amplitude (*A*: *r* = 0.57) and boundary separation (*b*: *r* = -0.54), conflating control-related processes with response caution. RT mean scores primarily recover non-decision time (*T*~er~: *r* = 0.83) but also boundary separation (*b*: *r* = 0.43), conflating perceptual encoding with decision caution. Accuracy mean scores strongly recover both boundary (*b*: *r* = 0.60) and controlled drift (μ~c~: *r* = 0.58), conflating caution with goal-directed processing.

This process impurity has critical implications for construct validity. When multiple processes jointly determine an indicator, individual differences in that indicator reflect a mixture of individual differences across multiple parameters. An individual showing high accuracy under conflict might have strong controlled processing, conservative response boundaries, weak automatic activation, or some combination. The behavioral indicator cannot disambiguate these alternatives. Interpreting such indicators as measuring single constructs ("attention control") oversimplifies the actual causal structure—the indicator conflates multiple distinct processes.

Process purity as the exception. Against this backdrop of typical confounding, RT difference scores demonstrate relatively *selective parameter recovery*—what we call process purity. RT difference strongly recovers automatic activation amplitude (*A*: *r* = 0.78) and moderately recovers temporal dynamics (τ: *r* = 0.36), while showing minimal recovery of other parameters (\|*r*\| ≤ 0.10 for μ~c~, *b*, and *T*~er~). Importantly, both *A* and τ represent control-related mechanisms within the DMC framework, albeit different aspects of control.

This pattern warrants careful interpretation. Although RT difference does not selectively recover the DMC's parameter for controlled drift (μ~c~: *r* = -0.10), it does selectively capture parameters reflecting interference control mechanisms. Recent theoretical work [@OberauerEtAl2024] distinguishes multiple facets of cognitive control: *proactive filtering* (preventing irrelevant information from influencing processing), *reactive suppression* (quickly removing automatic activation from the decision process), and *goal-directed processing* (top-down maintenance of task-relevant representations). Within the DMC architecture, automatic activation amplitude (*A*) reflects the strength of automatic response activation that must be controlled—lower *A* indicates better proactive filtering. Temporal dynamics (τ) reflects the rate at which this automatic activation is removed—higher τ indicates faster reactive suppression. Controlled drift (μ~c~) reflects goal-directed processing efficiency.

Thus, RT difference validly measures control-related mechanisms (proactive filtering and reactive suppression) while remaining relatively uncontaminated by non-control processes (encoding speed, response caution). It captures mechanisms 1 and 2 from the control fractionation framework, but not mechanism 3. This process purity is exceptional—a rare case where a simple behavioral indicator achieves selective measurement. The typical pattern, evident in the other three indicator types, is process impurity.

Equifinality and differential validity unified. This comprehensive view integrates what might initially appear as separate findings. *Equifinality*—the fact that multiple parameter combinations can generate similar behavioral patterns—manifests in the process impurity shown by most indicators. *Differential validity*—the fact that different indicators recover different parameters—reflects selective recovery patterns across the matrix. *Model-guided selection*—identifying optimal indicators for each parameter—emerges naturally from examining recovery strength. These are not separate phenomena but different perspectives on the same causal structure: behavioral indicators aggregate multiple process contributions in indicator-specific ways.

The decision-matrix view clarifies that no single indicator is universally "best." Validity is parameter-specific and construct-specific. If the research question targets proactive filtering and reactive suppression aspects of control, RT difference scores provide valid measurement. If the question targets response caution, accuracy mean scores are more appropriate (*b*: *r* = 0.60). If the question concerns goal-directed processing efficiency, accuracy mean (which recovers μ~c~: *r* = 0.58) may be preferable despite also recovering boundary (*b*: *r* = 0.60)—though the conflation complicates interpretation.

Importantly, this is not a failure of measurement precision. Even with perfect reliability, these recovery patterns would persist because they reflect the causal architecture of how processes generate behavior. The problem is not psychometric—it is theoretical and structural. Without a model specifying how multiple processes jointly contribute to behavior, we cannot determine which processes drive individual differences in any given indicator. Model-based validity analysis makes these relationships explicit and quantifiable.

These patterns help explain the weak cross-task correlations observed in empirical research [@HedgeEtAl2018]. If different tasks or different scoring methods differentially tap different process parameters, weak correlations may reflect differential validity rather than unreliability or construct distinctiveness. Two tasks might both involve conflict resolution, but if one task's indicators primarily capture automatic activation while another's capture controlled processing, behavioral correlations will be weak despite shared processing architecture.

### The Reliability Paradox Explained

![Dissociation between reliability and parameter recovery. Mean scores show high reliability but weak validity, while difference scores show moderate reliability but strong validity for conflict-specific parameters.](../figures/manuscript/DMC_Reliability_Paradox.png)

Figure 2 presents a particularly striking pattern that we term the "reliability paradox": Mean RT and accuracy scores show extremely high split-half reliability (*r* ≈ .99) but weak parameter recovery (\|*r*\| \< .30 for conflict-specific parameters). Conversely, difference scores show moderate to low reliability (*r* ≈ .20 - .70) but strong parameter recovery for control-related parameters (\|*r*\| \> .35 for *A* and τ in RT difference).

This dissociation directly challenges a fundamental assumption in psychometric theory: that reliability is necessary and nearly sufficient for validity. The high-reliability/low-recovery pattern for mean scores occurs because these indicators primarily reflect stable individual differences in general processing speed—a process component that is highly consistent but largely unrelated to the conflict-specific parameters of theoretical interest. Mean scores "work" psychometrically (they are reliable), but they don't measure what researchers typically intend to measure (conflict resolution processes).

The low-reliability/high-recovery pattern for difference scores occurs because removing the mean (subtracting congruent from incongruent performance) eliminates much stable individual-differences variance (general speed) while preserving variance related to conflict-specific processes. The resulting indicator is noisier (lower reliability) but more process-pure (higher validity for conflict parameters). This shows that optimizing reliability—a standard psychometric goal advocated in recent methodological work [@DraheimEtAl2021; @HedgeEtAl2018]—can actually reduce validity when it increases the influence of irrelevant processes relative to target processes.

This finding has important practical implications. Researchers facing the trade-off between reliable mean scores and noisier difference scores often choose mean scores for their superior psychometric properties. Our results suggest this choice may sacrifice validity for reliability when the goal is to measure process-specific individual differences rather than general performance level. The appropriate choice depends on what constructs the researcher aims to measure—an inherently theoretical question that psychometric properties alone cannot answer.

### Intermediate Modeling: No Universal Solution

![Comparison of parameter recovery between behavioral indicators and EZ-Diffusion Model estimates. Simplified computational modeling improves recovery for some parameters but not others, showing no universal advantage.](../figures/manuscript/DMC_Intermediate_Modeling.png)

Figure 3 compares parameter recovery for behavioral indicators versus estimates from a simplified computational model (EZ-Diffusion Model; @WagenmakersEtAl2007). The EZ-DM fits basic diffusion model parameters (drift, boundary, non-decision time) to aggregate behavioral statistics (mean RT, RT variance, accuracy) without assuming the DMC's dual-process architecture. This tests whether intermediate modeling—using simpler computational frameworks—improves validity over raw behavioral indicators.

Results show *mixed effectiveness*. For some DMC parameters, EZ-DM estimates achieve moderate recovery of boundary separation, performing comparably to accuracy-based behavioral indicators. However, for conflict-specific parameters, behavioral indicators often outperform EZ-DM estimates: RT difference scores show stronger recovery of automatic activation amplitude (*A*) than any EZ-DM parameter. Most strikingly, parameters specific to DMC's architecture—particularly amplitude (*A*) and temporal dynamics (τ)—show weak or negligible recovery from EZ-DM estimates because these parameters aren't represented in the EZ model's simpler architecture.

This pattern reveals an important constraint: model simplification does not automatically improve validity. Simpler models can be useful when they adequately capture relevant processes, but they fail when the generative architecture includes processes the simpler model doesn't represent. The appropriate model complexity depends on the complexity of the processes being measured—a theoretical question about cognitive architecture, not a purely methodological choice.

The implications extend beyond conflict tasks. Researchers sometimes hope that applying "any" computational model will improve measurement over behavioral indicators. Our results suggest this depends critically on whether the model's architecture matches the processes generating the data. Using an inadequate model may be worse than using well-chosen behavioral indicators, because the model imposes structure that misrepresents actual processes.

### Synthesis: What These Patterns Reveal

Together, these three analyses demonstrate systematic constraints on behavioral measurement that emerge from the structure of cognitive processing:

Process impurity is typical; process purity is exceptional. Most behavioral indicators aggregate multiple process contributions, creating confounding that no amount of psychometric refinement can eliminate. This is not a limitation of current methods—it reflects the causal architecture of how cognition generates behavior. Model-based validity analysis quantifies this impurity and identifies the rare exceptions (like RT difference scores for control-related parameters) where indicators achieve relative process purity. Figure 1's comprehensive heatmap shows that the typical pattern is process impurity—indicators conflating multiple mechanisms. RT difference's selective recovery of *A* and τ (both control-related) represents an unusual achievement of construct coherence at the indicator level.

Validity is parameter-specific and construct-dependent. Different indicators measure different processes, even when derived from the same task. "The task" does not have a single validity—validity depends on which process parameters are targeted, how the construct is theoretically fractionated, and which indicators are used. Claiming that a task measures "attention control" requires specifying which aspects of control (proactive filtering, reactive suppression, goal-directed processing) and demonstrating that chosen indicators validly capture those specific mechanisms. The control fractionation framework [@OberauerEtAl2024] illustrates why: these are dissociable processes that may require different measurement approaches.

Reliability does not guarantee validity. Indicators can be highly reliable while measuring irrelevant processes (mean scores capturing general speed and baseline accuracy), or modestly reliable while validly measuring target processes (difference scores capturing conflict-specific mechanisms). Psychometric optimization based on reliability may reduce validity when it increases the influence of nuisance processes relative to target processes. This reliability paradox challenges common measurement priorities and shows why validity assessment cannot rely on psychometric properties alone.

Theoretical models enable principled measurement. By specifying how processes generate behavior, models allow researchers to identify which indicators best measure which processes. This provides an alternative to either arbitrary indicator selection or purely correlation-based optimization. Parameter recovery analysis makes validity claims explicit, quantifiable, and testable. The comprehensive recovery matrix (Figure 1) provides actionable guidance: if measuring proactive filtering and reactive suppression, use RT difference; if measuring response caution, use accuracy mean; if measuring goal-directed processing, consider accuracy mean despite confounding with boundary.

Model complexity must match process complexity. Simplified models help when they capture relevant processes but mislead when they oversimplify. The appropriate level of model complexity is a theoretical question about processing architecture, not a statistical preference for parsimony. The EZ-Diffusion Model example shows that intermediate modeling is not universally superior—it depends on whether the simplified architecture adequately represents the processes of interest. When simplified models omit key mechanisms (like DMC's amplitude and temporal dynamics parameters), behavioral indicators may provide more valid measurement than model-based estimates.

These findings challenge common measurement assumptions while providing constructive guidance. Valid measurement requires explicit process theory, and validity assessment requires testing how indicators relate to process parameters under those theories. The next sections extend these findings by examining cross-task validity (Section 11) and articulating practical implications (Section 12).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Parameter Overlap and Cross-Task Validity

The findings presented so far focus on validity within single tasks—whether behavioral indicators successfully capture process parameters when data are generated from a single task architecture. However, a central goal of individual-differences research is to identify constructs that generalize across tasks. Researchers use multiple tasks presumed to measure the same construct (e.g., multiple conflict tasks all purportedly measuring "attention control"), expecting substantial convergent validity if the construct is coherent. Section 3 noted that such cross-task correlations are typically weak empirically. Our simulations reveal why: even when processes are perfectly shared across tasks, correlations among behavioral indicators substantially underestimate correlations among underlying parameters.

The convergent validity logic revisited. Traditional convergent validity assessment reasons as follows: if two tasks measure the same construct, individual differences in that construct should produce substantial cross-task correlations. Weak correlations are therefore interpreted as evidence that tasks don't measure the same construct—that apparent construct homogeneity is illusory. This reasoning assumes that construct-level correlations translate straightforwardly to indicator-level correlations, differing primarily by attenuation due to measurement error.

However, the differential validity patterns documented in Section 10 (particularly Figure 1's comprehensive recovery matrix) suggest this assumption may fail. If different indicators from nominally similar tasks differentially tap different process parameters due to slight task differences or scoring choices, then indicator-level correlations will be attenuated even when the underlying processes are perfectly shared. To test this, we conducted cross-task correlation simulations.

![Correlation transfer failure. Strong parameter correlations across tasks (r = 0.60-0.80) transfer weakly to behavioral indicator correlations (r = 0.0-0.40), showing that process-level construct coherence doesn't guarantee indicator-level convergent validity.](../figures/manuscript/DMC_Correlation_Transfer.png)

Figure 4 presents results from simulations where we generated data from two independent "tasks" (separate applications of the same DMC model), inducing known correlations between corresponding parameters across tasks. For example, we might set controlled drift in Task 1 to correlate *r* = .70 with controlled drift in Task 2, simulating individuals who show consistent controlled processing efficiency across tasks. We then computed cross-task correlations among behavioral indicators and compared them to the generating parameter correlations.

The results reveal dramatic attenuation. When parameter correlations are *r* = .60 to .80 (representing strong cross-task process consistency), corresponding behavioral indicator correlations range from *r* = .0 to .40. Even when the underlying construct is perfectly coherent at the parameter level—the same process with the same individual differences appears in both tasks—the behavioral indicators show what would typically be interpreted as weak or absent convergent validity.

This pattern holds across multiple parameter types and indicator types. Single-parameter correlations (e.g., only controlled drift correlated across tasks) transfer weakly, as do multi-parameter correlations (e.g., controlled drift, automatic activation, and temporal dynamics all correlated across tasks). RT indicators show somewhat better transfer than accuracy indicators, but all show substantial attenuation relative to the generating parameters.

Why correlation transfer fails. This attenuation reflects the confluence of several measurement realities documented earlier. First, process impurity (Figure 1) means each indicator reflects multiple parameters. If different parameters dominate in different tasks or conditions, indicator correlations will be weakened. Second, selective recovery patterns (Figure 1) mean different indicators tap different parameter blends. Unless indicator choices perfectly align across tasks, correlations will suffer. Third, measurement error (Figure 2) compounds these problems, though even reliability-corrected correlations show substantial attenuation.

The implication is striking: weak cross-task correlations do not necessarily indicate construct distinctiveness. They may reflect measurement limitations even when the underlying processes are perfectly shared. Conversely, moderate cross-task correlations might reflect only weak process overlap, with the rest driven by confounding from other shared processes (e.g., general processing speed).

Parameter-level constructs as the solution. These findings suggest that stable individual differences in cognitive processes should be located at the level of model parameters, not task scores. Rather than defining "attention control" as the common factor among conflict task scores, we might define it as shared individual differences in specific process parameters (e.g., controlled drift rate) estimated from conflict task models. This grounds construct definition in explicit process theory rather than empirical covariance.

This approach has been explored in recent work using hierarchical Bayesian modeling [@Vandekerckhove2014; @FrischkornEtAl2022]. In such models, parameters for each task are modeled as draws from higher-level distributions representing stable individual traits. For example, an individual's controlled drift parameter in a Flanker task and their controlled drift parameter in a Simon task might both be influenced by an underlying "controlled processing efficiency" trait. Whether such trait-level parameters meaningfully explain between-task covariance is an empirical question—one that can be addressed through hierarchical model comparison rather than assumed a priori.

This framework also clarifies what cross-task generalization means theoretically. Rather than assuming that high behavioral correlations demonstrate shared constructs, we explicitly model parameter sharing and test whether shared parameters improve model fit and prediction. When parameters do covary across tasks, we have evidence for construct coherence grounded in explicit process theory. When they don't, we learn that apparently similar tasks engage different processing mechanisms or that individual differences are context-specific.

Implications for construct validation. These findings have practical implications for how researchers establish and evaluate cognitive constructs:

*Task selection requires process analysis.* If the goal is to measure a specific process (e.g., controlled processing), simply selecting multiple tasks that "seem like" they require that process is insufficient. Researchers must analyze—ideally through modeling—whether the targeted process makes similar contributions to each task and each indicator.

*Factor models may mislead.* Extracting latent factors from behavioral indicators and interpreting them as representing cognitive processes can be misleading if indicators conflate processes differently across tasks. The factor might primarily reflect whatever process contributions happen to align across the chosen tasks and indicators, which may not correspond to the theoretical construct of interest.

*Parameter-level constructs provide principled alternatives.* Defining constructs at the parameter level, using hierarchical models to estimate cross-task parameter coherence, grounds construct building in explicit process theory. This doesn't eliminate all uncertainty, but it makes theoretical commitments transparent and testable.

*Weak convergent validity deserves reinterpretation.* Historical evidence of weak cross-task correlations in attention control, working memory, and executive function research should not necessarily be interpreted as evidence against construct coherence. It may reflect the measurement challenges documented here. Reevaluating construct coherence using parameter-level analyses might reveal stronger consistency than indicator-level analyses suggest.

Cross-domain applications. The correlation transfer problem extends beyond conflict tasks to fundamental measurement questions across cognitive psychology:

*Working memory capacity.* The persistent puzzle of why complex span tasks and N-back tasks—both labeled "working memory capacity" measures—correlate weakly [@BurgoyneEtAl2024] may reflect correlation transfer failure rather than construct distinctiveness. If span tasks emphasize memory maintenance and retrieval processes while N-back emphasizes updating and interference control, parameter-level analyses could reveal whether these processes are genuinely distinct or whether weak behavioral correlations mask shared parameter-level coherence. @UnsworthEngle2007 proposed that span performance reflects both primary memory (maintenance) and secondary memory (retrieval) contributions. Parameter recovery could test which processes each task emphasizes and whether their weak correlation reflects differential emphasis on shared processes or genuine process distinctiveness.

*Processing speed.* @SchmitzWilhelm2016 showed that different distributional parameters of RT (mean, variance, skew) relate differently to intelligence and show different developmental trajectories, suggesting they reflect distinct processes. Diffusion modeling reveals these map onto theoretically interpretable parameters (drift rate, boundary, non-decision time). Cross-task correlations among simple RT measures may substantially underestimate process-level coherence in speed-related parameters, explaining why behavioral speed measures sometimes show weaker construct coherence than expected.

*Cross-domain construct claims.* Claims that "working memory capacity is attention control" [@KaneEngle2003; @KaneEtAl2001] rest on moderate behavioral correlations (*r* ≈ .3-.5) between WM and attention tasks. Our findings suggest this evidence is ambiguous: such correlations could reflect strong parameter-level overlap with weak indicator validity, weak parameter-level overlap with moderate indicator validity, or shared method variance rather than shared cognitive processes. Parameter-level analyses testing whether specific WM parameters (e.g., maintenance capacity) correlate with specific attention parameters (e.g., controlled processing efficiency) could clarify what, if anything, is genuinely shared across these domains. Hierarchical Bayesian models [@UnsworthEtAl2004; @FrischkornEtAl2022] provide the framework for such tests, grounding cross-domain construct claims in explicit process theory rather than correlational patterns.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Measurement Implications

From Diagnosis to Prescription

The validity problems documented in Sections 3 and 10-11 might seem to counsel pessimism—if behavioral indicators conflate processes, if reliability doesn't guarantee validity, and if cross-task correlations dramatically underestimate process coherence, what can researchers do? This section articulates constructive implications, providing tiered recommendations that acknowledge varying research contexts, resources, and theoretical maturity while maintaining principled approaches to validity.

Three levels of measurement rigor. We propose a three-tiered framework matching measurement approaches to research goals and available resources:

Level 1: Minimal Standards (Acknowledge Limitations). When formal modeling is not feasible—due to limited resources, preliminary research stages, or applied contexts requiring practical measures—researchers should at minimum:

-   *Report multiple indicators* from each task rather than selecting a single "best" measure, recognizing that different indicators may capture different process blends.
-   *Avoid strong construct claims* without explicit process theory. Refer to measures as "task performance" or "behavioral effects" rather than labeling them as direct measures of specific cognitive processes.
-   *Interpret cautiously*, acknowledging that correlational patterns alone cannot establish what processes drive performance.
-   *Consider convergent validity patterns as informative but insufficient*, recognizing that weak correlations may reflect measurement issues rather than construct problems, while strong correlations don't guarantee construct coherence.

This level represents honest measurement practice that doesn't overstate what purely behavioral indicators can establish. It allows productive empirical research while maintaining epistemic humility about validity claims.

Level 2: Good Practice (Semi-Formal Process Analysis). When resources permit more intensive investigation, researchers can strengthen validity claims through systematic process decomposition even without complete formal models:

-   *Explicit process decomposition*: Identify hypothesized component processes theoretically, following approaches like @FrischkornOberauer2025 with antisaccade tasks or @FriedmanMiyake2004 with executive functions.
-   *Systematic manipulation*: Design experiments that selectively affect proposed process components, deriving testable predictions about which manipulations should affect which indicators.
-   *Test alternative process explanations*: Consider and empirically evaluate whether observed patterns could arise from processes other than those hypothesized.
-   *Report patterns inconsistent with process assumptions*, treating contradictions as opportunities for theoretical refinement rather than nuisances to explain away.
-   *Use multiple tasks strategically*: Select tasks predicted to engage target processes differently based on explicit theory, rather than assuming all "similar" tasks measure the same construct.

This level provides substantial constraints on interpretations and can falsify oversimplified task-process mappings. It represents significant progress over purely correlational validation while remaining accessible to laboratories without extensive computational modeling expertise. The @FrischkornOberauer2025 antisaccade work exemplifies this approach, showing how systematic experimentation and explicit process theorizing constrain validity claims.

Level 3: Gold Standard (Generative Modeling and Parameter Recovery). When measuring specific process parameters is central to research goals—particularly for individual-differences claims about cognitive mechanisms—full generative modeling provides the strongest validity foundation:

-   *Specify complete generative models* formalizing theory about how processes operate and generate data.
-   *Conduct parameter recovery analyses* (as demonstrated in Section 10) to identify which indicators optimally capture which parameters.
-   *Test model adequacy* through posterior predictive checks, testing qualitative patterns the model should produce, and comparing competing models.
-   *Use hierarchical parameter estimation* [@Vandekerckhove2014; @RouderHaaf2019] to model individual differences at the parameter level, distinguishing stable traits from context-specific variation.
-   *Report parameter-level constructs* as the basis for individual-differences claims, with behavioral indicators serving as observable manifestations of underlying parameters.
-   *Evaluate cross-task parameter coherence* explicitly through hierarchical models, testing whether parameter correlations support construct generalization.

This level provides the strongest validity evidence and enables the most precise construct-level claims. While it requires substantial investment in model development, estimation, and validation, tools are increasingly accessible (e.g., HDDM, @WieckiEtAl2013; Bayesian cognitive modeling packages, @LeeWagenmakers2013), and the cognitive modeling community has developed extensive methodological resources.

Applying model-guided measurement across domains. The parameter recovery framework demonstrated in Section 10 (Figure 1) provides actionable guidance extending beyond conflict tasks:

*Working memory.* Parameter recovery analyses could resolve ongoing debates about what working memory tasks measure [@WilhelmEtAl2013; @WilhelmEtAl2025; @BurgoyneEtAl2024]. Do complex span tasks primarily measure maintenance capacity, retrieval efficiency, or attentional control? Do N-back tasks measure updating, interference control, or maintenance? Parameter recovery testing which span indicators (e.g., total correct, longest sequence, partial credit scoring) best recover which memory process parameters (maintenance, retrieval, updating) would ground indicator selection in explicit theory. This could explain why different WM tasks correlate weakly: they may emphasize different component processes, all legitimately labeled "working memory" but requiring different measurement approaches.

*Processing speed.* Diffusion modeling applications [@SchmitzWilhelm2016; @SchubertEtAl2018] show that different RT distributional parameters recover different cognitive processes. Parameter recovery could guide whether researchers should analyze RT means (primarily reflecting drift rate and non-decision time), RT variance (reflecting trial-to-trial variability in drift), or RT distributional shape (reflecting boundary and non-decision time mixtures). This resolves Donders' original decomposition question [@Donders1868] through empirical parameter recovery rather than subtraction logic.

*Cross-domain integration.* Parameter-level analyses enable principled tests of cross-domain construct claims. Rather than inferring that "WMC is attention control" from moderate behavioral correlations [@KaneEngle2003], hierarchical models could test whether WM maintenance parameters correlate with attention control parameters, whether retrieval parameters correlate with controlled processing, and which processes are shared versus domain-specific. This transforms conceptual debates into empirical questions with explicit theoretical commitments.

Matching rigor to research goals. These levels are not a hierarchy of "good" versus "bad" research—they represent matches between measurement standards and research purposes:

-   *Exploratory research identifying relevant variables* can proceed at Level 1, using behavioral indicators to map phenomena while acknowledging limited validity claims.
-   *Process-theoretic research testing mechanisms* benefits from Level 2, using systematic manipulation and process decomposition to constrain interpretations.
-   *Individual-differences research making claims about specific cognitive mechanisms* requires Level 3, using formal models to ground measurement in explicit process theory.

The key principle is that the strength of validity claims should match the strength of theoretical and empirical justification. Stronger claims require stronger evidence.

Practical barriers and solutions. Several practical concerns commonly arise regarding formal modeling approaches:

*"Formal models are too complex and resource-intensive."* Fair point—but (1) semi-formal approaches (Level 2) often suffice and are quite accessible, (2) many existing models can be applied without developing new ones, (3) parameter recovery analyses need not be conducted for every study—they establish validity once for an indicator-construct pairing, which informs subsequent research. Moreover, investing resources in validity rather than simply collecting more data with questionable measures may be more efficient long-term.

*"Model choice seems arbitrary—different models give different answers."* This is model-relativity (Section 6), which we reframe as epistemic progress: models make assumptions explicit and testable. When models disagree, this clarifies what theoretical commitments matter, guiding empirical resolution. This beats having hidden disagreements embedded in implicit assumptions underlying behavioral indicators.

*"This invalidates decades of existing research."* Not quite—it clarifies what existing evidence can and cannot establish. Correlational patterns remain informative about nomological networks; they simply don't establish construct representation without additional theoretical work. Much existing research provides valuable phenomena to explain and theoretical insights to formalize. The challenge is moving forward with stronger validity foundations, not dismissing past contributions.

*"Not all constructs can be formalized."* True—some constructs may resist formalization at present. But this reflects real scientific uncertainty about what the construct is and how it operates. Acknowledging this honestly is better than pretending behavioral indicators have established validity. Moreover, the formalization continuum (Section 5) shows that partial formalization can strengthen validity claims even when complete models aren't achievable.

Intermediate modeling: promises and limits. Section 10 showed that simplified computational models (e.g., EZ-Diffusion Model) don't universally improve validity over behavioral indicators. When should researchers use intermediate models?

*Use simplified models when*: (1) They adequately represent the key processes of interest, even if they omit details. (2) Full models are computationally intractable but simplified versions capture core mechanisms. (3) The research goal is to separate broad process categories (e.g., speed vs. caution) rather than measure fine-grained parameters.

*Avoid simplified models when*: (1) They omit processes central to the construct of interest (as EZ-DM omits DMC's conflict-specific parameters). (2) They impose architectural assumptions known to be violated in the task context. (3) Behavioral indicators already capture the target processes as well as the simplified model would.

The principle is architectural adequacy: models must represent the processes they claim to measure. Simpler is not automatically better if simplicity omits what matters.

Recommendations for attention control research specifically. Applying these principles to the attention control domain that motivated our analysis:

-   Acknowledge multi-component nature: "Attention control" comprises multiple dissociable processes (conflict detection, resolution dynamics, distractor suppression, strategy adjustment). Different tasks and indicators tap these differently.
-   Report multiple indicators: Mean performance, condition-specific performance, and difference scores all provide information; none is universally best.
-   Use process models when possible: Fit cognitive models (DMC, SSP, or alternatives) to isolate process contributions, reporting parameter-level individual differences.
-   Interpret factor structures cautiously: Latent factors from attention tasks may primarily reflect general speed or caution rather than control-specific processes. Test this through parameter-based analyses.
-   Reconsider weak convergent validity: Before concluding attention control is incoherent, test whether parameter-level coherence exceeds indicator-level convergent validity, as our simulations suggest is likely.

These recommendations aim to move attention control research toward validity claims grounded in explicit process theory while acknowledging practical constraints.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Model Dependence and Generalizability

All validity claims presented thus far are conditional on the correctness of the process models we employed—primarily the Diffusion Model for Conflict (DMC). A critic might reasonably ask: don't these findings simply reflect artifacts of DMC's specific architecture? Would different models yield different conclusions? This section addresses these concerns by examining results from the Shrinking Spotlight model (SSP), which embodies different theoretical assumptions about conflict processing, and by clarifying what generalizes across models versus what remains model-specific.

Parallel findings from SSP simulations. We conducted identical parameter recovery analyses using the Shrinking Spotlight model [@WhiteEtAl2011], which conceptualizes conflict quite differently from DMC. Rather than emphasizing temporal dynamics of automatic versus controlled processing, SSP focuses on spatial attention—conflict arises from initially broad attentional focus that gradually narrows. The model includes different parameters (perceptual input *p*, initial attentional spread *sd_0*) representing different process mechanisms. If our findings merely reflected DMC's architectural idiosyncrasies, we would expect qualitatively different patterns under SSP.

Instead, we observe remarkable convergence (Supplementary Figures 1-6):

-   Parameter confounding (Supp. Fig. 1) appears in SSP just as in DMC, with recovery correlations between parameters ranging from *r* = -.35 to .55. Multiple parameter combinations generate similar behavioral patterns, creating equifinality.

-   Reliability paradox (Supp. Fig. 2) replicates: mean scores show high reliability (.95+) but weak parameter recovery, while difference scores show moderate reliability (.30-.70) but stronger recovery for SSP-specific parameters like initial attentional spread (*sd_0*: *r* ≈ .72 for PC difference scores).

-   Differential validity (Supp. Fig. 3) generalizes: RT indicators preferentially recover perceptual input parameters (*p*: *r* ≈ .59 for RT mean), while accuracy indicators recover attentional focus parameters (*sd_0*: *r* ≈ .72 for PC difference). Different indicators measure different processes under SSP's architecture, just as under DMC.

-   Correlation transfer failure (Supp. Fig. 4) occurs similarly: parameter correlations of *r* = .60-.80 transfer to behavioral correlations of only *r* = .10-.45.

-   Model-guided selection (Supp. Fig. 5) reveals SSP-specific optimal indicators: PC difference scores strongly recover initial attentional spread, while RT mean scores best recover perceptual input. The specific indicator-parameter mappings differ from DMC (as expected given different parameters), but the principle that optimal indicators differ by parameter holds consistently.

-   Intermediate modeling limits (Supp. Fig. 6) appear even more starkly: EZ-Diffusion Model parameters show poor recovery of SSP-specific mechanisms. EZ-DM cannot adequately capture attentional spotlight dynamics, so its estimates provide weaker validity than carefully chosen behavioral indicators for SSP-specific parameters.

What generalizes versus what's model-specific. This pattern clarifies which findings reflect general measurement constraints versus particular theoretical commitments:

Architectural-general principles: - Behavioral indicators conflate multiple process contributions (equifinality) - Different indicators show parameter-specific validity - Reliability doesn't guarantee validity; validity depends on process selectivity - Cross-task parameter correlations transfer weakly to indicator correlations - Model complexity must match the process architecture being measured

These patterns emerge from both DMC and SSP despite different processing assumptions, suggesting they reflect fundamental constraints on behavioral measurement rather than model artifacts. The causes are similar: behavioral data aggregate contributions from multiple processes operating simultaneously, and separating these contributions requires explicit models of how they jointly generate observations.

Model-specific findings: - *Which* parameters are most recoverable - *Which* indicators optimally measure *which* parameters - Quantitative recovery correlations

These model-specific results appropriately differ because DMC and SSP theorize different process mechanisms. DMC's controlled drift (*mu_c*) and temporal dynamics (*tau*) have no direct counterparts in SSP, while SSP's attentional spread (*sd_0*) has no direct DMC analog. Each model identifies parameter-indicator mappings consistent with its process theory. This is not a limitation—it's exactly what model-relativity entails (Section 6).

Implications for model-relativity concerns. The convergence of architectural-general principles across models addresses a key worry about model-relativity: if validity depends on models, and models are underdetermined by data, doesn't this make validity arbitrary? Three points respond to this concern:

First, *convergent principles across models demonstrate robustness*. When different models with different architectures all reveal parameter confounding, differential validity, and reliability-validity dissociations, these patterns likely reflect genuine constraints on measurement rather than model artifacts. The principles are empirically robust even as specific applications remain model-relative.

Second, *model-specific findings appropriately differ*. When different models make different predictions about indicator validity, this reflects different theoretical commitments. Testing which predictions better match empirical data contributes to model evaluation and theoretical progress. This is how science works—competing theories make different predictions that can be empirically adjudicated.

Third, *model adequacy is independently testable*. Validity claims conditional on a model remain valuable if the model is empirically adequate—if it accounts for qualitative patterns, predicts novel phenomena, and outperforms competitors. Model testing and model-based validity assessment proceed in parallel, mutually constraining each other.

Generalization beyond conflict tasks. Do these principles extend to other task domains? While we cannot demonstrate this conclusively without further simulations, several considerations suggest broad applicability:

*The problems are architectural.* Equifinality, differential validity, and reliability-validity dissociations arise from general features of cognitive processing: multiple processes operate simultaneously, different observable outcomes are differentially sensitive to different processes, and aggregating over conditions affects which processes dominate indicators. These features characterize most cognitive tasks, not just conflict paradigms.

*Analogous problems appear elsewhere.* Working memory tasks show similar validity challenges: weak cross-task correlations despite presumed shared constructs [@OberauerEtAl2024], debates about what latent factors represent, uncertainty about indicator selection. Decision-making tasks face equifinality problems when multiple accumulator parameters can produce similar behavior [@DonkinEtAl2015]. These suggest measurement challenges we identified are not conflict-specific.

*The solutions are domain-general.* Parameter recovery logic applies to any generative model. Semi-formal process decomposition can clarify validity in any task where processes can be theoretically specified and experimentally isolated. The three-tiered framework (Section 12) translates to any domain where process-based constructs are measured through behavior.

Nevertheless, domain-specific work remains necessary. Each task domain has specific theoretical models, specific validity questions, and specific practical constraints. The framework we provide is general, but its application requires domain-appropriate theoretical and empirical work.

The value of multiple models. Rather than viewing competing models as a problem, we propose embracing theoretical pluralism. Multiple models serve several functions:

-   Robustness testing: Findings replicating across models are more likely to be general principles rather than artifacts.
-   Boundary condition identification: Where models diverge reveals which aspects of validity depend on specific architectural assumptions.
-   Theoretical progress: Model comparison—testing which architectural assumptions better account for data—advances understanding of cognitive processes themselves, not just their measurement.

The SSP-DMC comparison demonstrates this approach. Both models reveal similar general principles while differing in specific predictions. These differences aren't weaknesses—they clarify exactly what theoretical commitments matter for validity claims and what evidence would favor one model over another.

Conclusion regarding model-dependence. Model-relativity is real—validity claims depend on theoretical assumptions about processing architecture. But this dependence represents intellectual honesty rather than nihilism. The alternative—treating behavioral indicators as theory-neutral measurements—simply hides theoretical assumptions rather than eliminating them. By making assumptions explicit through formal models, testing model adequacy, comparing competing models, and identifying architectural-general principles, we can make genuine progress in understanding both cognitive processes and their valid measurement. The convergence of findings across DMC and SSP demonstrates that this progress is achievable.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Conclusion

Validity Requires Theory

This paper began with three parallel measurement puzzles: the attention control puzzle (toolbox approaches yielding weak convergent validity), the working memory capacity puzzle (span and N-back tasks correlating weakly), and the processing speed puzzle (RT measures conflating multiple temporal processes). We argued that these are not isolated challenges but symptoms of a structural problem: validity claims require explicit models of how cognitive processes generate behavioral data, and such models have often remained implicit or absent across cognitive domains. The sophisticated psychometric methods cannot resolve what measurements mean without theoretical specification of what cognitive mechanisms produce them.

Our analysis proceeded through three phases. First, we diagnosed why traditional validation approaches based on cross-task correlations and factor structures encounter fundamental limits. These methods cannot escape circularity: when tasks are validated by their relations to other tasks presumed to measure the same construct, the construct interpretation presupposes what the validation ostensibly establishes. Second, we developed a model-based framework for validity assessment, showing how explicit process theories—ranging from semi-formal decompositions to fully generative computational models—enable validity claims grounded in testable theoretical commitments rather than correlational patterns alone. Third, we demonstrated this framework through systematic simulations of conflict task performance, revealing phenomena that clarify indicator meaning: parameter confounding showing that indicators aggregate multiple processes, differential validity demonstrating parameter-specific indicator sensitivity, reliability paradoxes revealing validity-reliability dissociations, correlation transfer failures explaining weak convergent validity despite process coherence, and intermediate modeling limits showing that simplified models don't universally improve validity.

These findings lead to a clear conclusion: *validity in cognitive psychology is fundamentally a theoretical achievement, not a psychometric property*. It cannot be established through reliability coefficients, factor loadings, or convergent validity correlations alone, no matter how sophisticated the psychometric modeling. Validity requires explicit commitments about what cognitive processes operate and how they generate observable behavior—commitments that can be formalized with varying degrees of precision but cannot be avoided.

What this framework provides. The computational psychometrics approach we advocate offers several advantages over purely correlational validation:

*Explicit theoretical commitments.* Formal models make all assumptions visible: what processes operate, how they interact, how they generate data. This enables critical evaluation that implicit theories resist.

*Independent validity criteria.* Parameter recovery provides validity evidence based on whether indicators track the processes that generated data, not on correlations with other potentially invalid measures.

*Quantitative validity assessment.* Recovery correlations estimate indicator validity for specific parameters, enabling principled comparison of measurement alternatives.

*Process specificity.* Rather than treating constructs as unitary, models distinguish component processes with potentially different individual-differences structures and different optimal indicators.

*Testable predictions.* Model-based validity claims are falsifiable: models can be tested against data, compared to competitors, and refined when inadequate. Correlational validity claims are harder to falsify because failure can always be attributed to imperfect measurement of the criterion construct.

*Cumulative theoretical progress.* As models improve through testing and refinement, validity claims become increasingly grounded in empirically adequate theories rather than arbitrary correlational assumptions.

Addressing concerns and limitations. Several concerns about this framework deserve direct response:

*Concern: "Formal models are too complex and resource-intensive for most research."* Response: Semi-formal approaches (Section 5) and Level 2 recommendations (Section 12) often suffice for strengthening validity claims without full computational modeling. Moreover, validity investment can be more efficient than collecting extensive data with questionable measures. Finally, modeling tools are increasingly accessible (HDDM, Bayesian cognitive modeling packages), and the research community can share modeling work—parameter recovery for conflict tasks need not be replicated by every laboratory.

*Concern: "Model choice seems arbitrary; different models make different validity claims."* Response: This is model-relativity (Section 6), which we reframe as transparency rather than arbitrariness. Different models embody different theories and thus appropriately make different validity claims. The models themselves can be compared empirically. This situation is better than hidden theoretical disagreements embedded in implicit assumptions underlying "theory-neutral" behavioral indicators.

*Concern: "This seems to invalidate decades of cognitive psychology research."* Response: Not invalidate—reinterpret. Existing correlational evidence informs nomothetic networks and identifies phenomena requiring explanation. Existing theories provide process hypotheses to formalize. The challenge is building stronger validity foundations going forward, not dismissing past contributions. Much existing research provides valuable starting points for model-based reanalysis.

*Concern: "Some constructs may resist formalization."* Response: This may be true, but it reflects genuine scientific uncertainty about what the construct is and how it operates. Acknowledging this is better than treating behavioral indicators as having established validity by default. Moreover, partial formalization strengthens validity claims even when complete models aren't achievable yet.

*Concern: "Parameter recovery shows what's valid* given\* a model, but how do we know the model is right?"\* Response: Model adequacy is independently testable through standard methods—qualitative prediction testing, quantitative fit assessment, posterior predictive checks, model comparison. Model-based validity claims remain useful when models are empirically adequate, and they clarify what evidence would challenge both model and validity claims. This makes scientific progress possible through iterative model refinement.

Future directions. The framework we developed opens several productive research directions:

*Extension to other cognitive domains.* Apply parameter recovery logic to working memory tasks, decision-making paradigms, learning contexts, and other domains where processes must be inferred from behavior. Each domain will have specific models and challenges, but the general logic applies broadly.

*Development of accessible tools.* Create user-friendly software for conducting parameter recovery analyses, making these methods accessible to researchers without specialized computational skills. The ComputationalValidity R package provides a starting point.

*Integration with neural measures.* Combine behavioral parameter estimates with neural data (EEG, fMRI) to test whether model parameters correspond to distinct neural mechanisms, providing converging evidence for process decomposition.

*Hierarchical parameter-based constructs.* Extend hierarchical modeling approaches to define cognitive constructs at the parameter level across multiple tasks, testing whether parameter-based factors provide more coherent individual-differences structures than behavioral factors.

*Cross-cultural and developmental applications.* Test whether process parameters show different developmental trajectories or cultural variations than behavioral indicators, revealing developmental or cultural effects obscured by aggregate measures.

*Applied contexts.* Explore whether parameter-based cognitive assessment provides better prediction of real-world outcomes (academic performance, job performance, clinical symptoms) than behavioral indicators, testing practical utility of theoretically grounded measurement.

The path forward. Advancing validity in cognitive psychology requires moving beyond the assumption that better correlations, larger samples, or more sophisticated factor models will resolve fundamental validity questions. These contributions remain valuable—reliable measurement and appropriate statistical modeling are necessary—but they are insufficient without explicit process theory. The path forward requires:

*Theoretical investment* in developing, testing, and refining cognitive process models. This includes both completely formal models and semi-formal frameworks that systematically decompose processes.

*Measurement validation* based on process theory rather than correlational patterns alone. Parameter recovery provides one rigorous approach; semi-formal systematic manipulation provides another.

*Construct definition* grounded in process mechanisms rather than task covariance. Individual differences research should increasingly focus on parameter-level constructs that represent theoretically meaningful process properties.

*Epistemic humility* about what current measurement achieves. When process theory remains weak, validity claims should be appropriately cautious rather than assumed by default.

*Community standards* that recognize validity as requiring theoretical justification. Establishing validity should involve theoretical argumentation and empirical testing, not merely reporting reliability and factor loadings.

Final message. Validity is not a psychometric property that tasks possess or lack—it is a theoretical claim about relationships between processes and observations. Establishing validity requires theoretical work: specifying what processes operate, deriving testable predictions about how they generate data, and empirically evaluating whether indicators track process variation. Computational psychometrics provides a rigorous framework for this enterprise, but the fundamental requirement is intellectual: clear thinking about what we claim to measure and why we believe measurements reflect those claims.

The persistent validity challenges in cognitive psychology reflect neither methodological failure nor hopeless complexity. They reflect the inherent difficulty of inferring unobservable processes from noisy behavioral observations—a problem that cannot be solved by statistical sophistication alone but requires explicit theoretical commitments. Making those commitments transparent, testable, and subject to empirical constraint represents not a retreat from objectivity but an advance toward it. As @CronbachMeehl1955 recognized seven decades ago, construct validity is fundamentally about theory testing. The formal modeling tools now available make that theory testing more rigorous and precise than ever before.

The cognitive tasks researchers develop and refine are powerful tools for understanding the mind. But tools require users who understand their proper application and limits. Behavioral indicators are not transparent windows into cognitive processes—they are complex aggregates produced by multiple mechanisms operating simultaneously. Understanding what our measurements mean requires understanding the processes that generate them. That understanding comes not from correlations, but from theory.

Advancing validity in cognitive psychology requires not better correlations, but better theories of how cognition generates data.
